column1: Text

Ever since cars got super popular in the early 1900s, thanks to Henry Ford and his Model T, we've been using them all the time. But now, people are wondering if maybe we should use cars less. Honestly, I think it could be a good idea!

Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," makes a good point. Cars are a huge deal in the suburbs, where lots of middle-class families live, like in Shanghai or Chicago. Experts say this car thing makes it hard to cut down on pollution from vehicles. In Europe, cars make up 12% of that pollution, but in the US, where we drive everywhere, it can be as high as 50%! The more we drive, the more pollution we make. Also, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," talks about how Paris had to ban cars because the air was so polluted. One Monday, cars with even numbers on their license plates weren't allowed to drive, and the next day, it was odd numbers. Cars are a big part of why cities like Paris have so much pollution, which shows how bad they are for the environment.

Then there's Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It's about how millions of people in Colombia ditched their cars for a day and walked, biked, or took the bus. The streets in the capital were super quiet! This happens every year, and no private cars are allowed, just buses and taxis in the city of seven million people. People like car-free days because they cut down on pollution from cars. The article also mentions how they've made the city better, with new parks, sports centers, sidewalks, less traffic, and new shops and restaurants. Less traffic has really helped Colombia fix things up, make traffic easier, and get new businesses going.

So, bottom line, using fewer cars and having car-free days has really made cities cleaner and less polluted. It's also helped countries like Colombia fix their roads and ease traffic. I think it could be good for America too! Maybe we could bike or walk more for short trips instead of always driving. Personally, I think cutting back on car use is a smart move.

Across the world, most countries require transportation. Cars, buses, and other forms of transportation help make travel easier and faster. However, this easiness also leads to pollution problems. Mobile transportation is part of our daily lives, but our reliance on cars also contributes to harmful greenhouse gas emissions that we could reduce.

Vauban, a small town in Germany, has begun using a "car-free" way of life. Here, important things such as grocery stores are placed close to where people live, instead of far away on highways. While cars are still around, 70% of families living in Vauban do not own cars. Strangely, 57% of families said that they sold their cars just to move to Vauban. Some residents say that they feel less stressed because they rely less on cars. Cars contribute about 12% of the world's greenhouse gas emissions, which can increase to 50% in car-dependent areas of the United States.

Paris is another interesting example of how reducing car usage can help with smog reduction. When there was a bad smog event, Parisian officials came up with a plan to reduce pollution levels. On Mondays, cars with license plates ending in even numbers were not allowed to drive, or they would be fined. The same rule occurred on Tuesdays, but only cars with odd-numbered license plates were banned. This decision led to a 60% decrease in traffic within five days. Diesel fuel was a big part of the pollution problem since 67% of vehicles in France use diesel. However, the effects of removing the smog were lessened when the Tuesday ban on odd-numbered plates was stopped.

Can you think of a day with no cars at all? This actually occurs once a year in Bogota, Colombia. Private vehicles are not driven for the day, except for buses and taxis. Even a single car-free day can help lower pollution a bit. This yearly event is part of a bigger campaign that started in Bogota in the mid-1990s. The campaign has helped create many new bicycle paths and sidewalks all over the city. Parks and recreation centers have also appeared. While living a car-free lifestyle can be hard, it also has benefits.

In short, getting rid of cars completely might not be possible in today's world. However, countries should reduce car usage and pollution. It would greatly affect the world if countries around the world worked together to prioritize reducing air pollution. Mobile transportation is part of our daily lives, but relying too much on cars and other vehicles can negatively impact the planet's health and our own health.

Elisabeth Rosenthal thinks America might not love cars as much anymore. She's basically saying people are using cars less and less. This could be a good thing in some ways. If we drive less, it could be safer, healthier, and less polluting. We also wouldn't rely on cars so much.

First off, driving less could make us safer and healthier. Feeling safe is super important. Andrew Selsky said that on a car-free day in Colombia, the streets were strangely empty. Avoiding traffic jams can make you feel calm. If you're trying to get somewhere, traffic can mess things up and stress you out. Less driving could mean less traffic and less stress. Heidrun Walter said she used to be tense when she had a car, but now she's much happier without one. Plus, driving less encourages exercise. Cars are for getting around, but busy lives can make it hard to do fun stuff. So, less driving can lead to more activity. Selsky also mentioned that parks and sports centers were popping up all over the city. Fewer cars mean nicer, more natural places, and more parks and sports centers mean people get more exercise. Basically, driving less means less stress and better health.

Second, driving less is good for the planet. Some people don't care about the environment, but it's a real issue. Pollution is bad for the environment and makes us unhealthy. Cars cause a lot of pollution. Rosenthal says that driving less "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving means less pollution. Driving less is a chance to make the world cleaner and have a better future. Pollution is super serious, and drivers are partly responsible.

Also, we'd rely on cars less. Lots of people think cars are a must-have, but they don't realize how much they depend on them. Technology has changed how we live, and we've lost touch with nature. Depending on something isn't always good. David Goldberg says that "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some people might disagree, but being dependent on something that could be harmful might not be progress. Rosenthal said that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can afford a car. People who are used to cars have forgotten about nature and don't always think about the environment. Driving less could help with these problems.

In short, driving less could make us healthier, reduce pollution, and make us less dependent on cars. Driving less creates a cleaner, more organized world and keeps people healthy and safe. Maybe one reason America is doing well is that we're not driving as much, but we're focusing on what's best for everyone.

How often are you behind the wheel? Is a car your go-to for work, errands, or mall trips? Ever stop to think about how many others are doing the same – firing up their engines for every little thing? It's easy to miss the big environmental hit each time you turn the key. What if you skipped driving today? For many drivers, the first thought is, "No way! I need my car!" But, it's possible to cut back on car use. Cities like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have found ways to get people to drive less, and it's often worked out well. Driving less can actually bring a bunch of good things for you and everyone around you.

One surprising plus of driving less is how it can boost your mood. Heidrun Walter said, "He was always stressed with a car. Now, he's much happier without it." Think about all the road rage that comes with driving. That's not good for your head or your health. Driving takes a lot of focus and brainpower – figuring out routes, dealing with rude drivers, and so on – which can quickly lead to stress and tiredness. Big cities like New York and Paris often have crazy traffic and lots of pollution. Robert Duffer wrote in "Paris bans driving due to smog" that warm air can trap car fumes. That's definitely not healthy. Duffer also said that Paris had to ban cars for a bit when pollution got super bad. After just a few days of less driving, the air got way cleaner. When people can't drive as much, they find other ways to get around, like walking, biking, or skating. These things give you exercise. You get a workout and feel better mentally than just sitting in a car.

Cutting back on cars can also make your city look nicer. Pollution messes up plants and smells bad. It puts a dark cloud over everything, making it feel "dirty." Less driving means fewer nasty fumes, which means a cleaner place. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote in "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars" that less car traffic naturally leads to more people walking. More walkers mean more sidewalks and shops nearby. Andrew Selsky said, "Parks and sports centers have popped up all over the place... bumpy sidewalks have been replaced by smooth ones... and new restaurants and fancy stores have shown up." Like we said, pollution is bad for the environment. Groups like the Environmental Protection Agency are pushing for "car-reduced" areas, according to Rosenthal. These places have much less pollution and are cleaner in general. Cities are even doing "no-car" days. Bogotá, Colombia, has a "car-free" day every year where only buses and taxis are allowed; other drivers get fined. While fining people for using their own cars might be controversial, it's been pretty successful, and the smog goes down a lot. So, even though cutting back on cars and walking more might seem tough, it's not impossible. Driving less has lots of perks, like better health, nicer cities, and a cleaner environment for everyone.

Cars? Seriously amazing! Maybe *the* top invention, ever. They whisk us from place to place. Isn't that the whole point? We want to get there fast, and cars deliver! Cities shrink in minutes—nothing else compares. Sure, trains and buses exist, but cars *feel* better. But, hold on, maybe cars also bring tons of stress and mess with our planet. Buses and trains mean no car repairs, no traffic headaches. Plus, less pollution! It's not obvious, but less car use is a win.

It's a win for us *and* the planet. Less air pollution is a *huge* deal. Cars spew bad gases – greenhouse stuff – from their exhaust. One car? Not a biggie. But *all* the cars? That's a problem, especially in packed cities like Paris. They had *crazy* pollution! So, they banned some drivers. As Robert Duffer said in "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," license plates ending in even numbers had to stay home or pay up. Then, the next day, odd numbers! This cut traffic by 60%! Bogota, Colombia, does car-free days to fight smog, says Andrew Selsky in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota." They want people to try other ways to get around and clean up the air. More and more, people everywhere see how cutting back on cars helps the Earth.

Another thing? Less stress! Driving *is* stressful. Traffic jams, constant car washes, the noise – it all adds up. Less driving? Less stress! No more traffic, no more car repairs, no more cramped, noisy rides. Elisabeth Rosenthal, in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," quotes Hartmut Walter: "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." He says ditching the car made him happier, less stressed. Andrew Selsky, again in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota," says those days are "a good opportunity to take away stress..." If we can chill out more, why not? Less stress means a better life.

So, less car use helps the planet *and* our stress levels. The planet needs our help *badly*. And stress? It's terrible for us. If we want a better planet and less stress, this is a good way to do it. Some might not get it right away, but it's worked in lots of places. It's a powerful tool with awesome results.

The electoral college? It's just not fair! People can't directly elect their president. They pick electors, who *then* pick the president, be they Democrat or Republican. But get this: source two says those electors could ditch the popular vote! Talk about messing with democracy. We vote to pick someone to protect us and help us thrive, but this whole system messes that up. The big problem? The "winner-take-all" rule.

Right now, we don't directly pick our leader. Like source two says, we vote for electors, each state gets a set number. Then *they* decide who's president. Source two says there are 538 electors; you need 270 votes to win. A state's electors? That's how many folks it has in Congress. A fair system? One where every vote counts! No electoral college means every single vote adds up. Candidates get votes based on what people want, ties are rare. Right now, your vote feels worthless if your state mostly likes someone else. Get rid of the college, and suddenly, your vote matters!

Source two mentions "faithless" electors. These electors could vote for the other side! That hurts voters and puts America at risk. If a Republican elector votes Democrat, poof! The Republican votes mean nothing. We only get to pick a president every four years. Electors switching their vote? No way is that okay.

Sources two and three keep bringing up the "winner-take-all" thing. Lose, and your votes are trash. The winner gets everything! It's like those voters who picked the loser secretly wanted the winner.

So, state senators, dump the electoral college! It stinks. It puts some votes over others. It lets electors pick based on their own feelings. And that "winner-take-all" thing? It throws away votes for the losers.

Dear Senator, I'm writing to ask for a rethink of how we pick our president. Moving away from the Electoral College to something that better shows what people want is important. Right now, it's unfair because electors could change the overall vote of Americans.

Changing the Electoral College might seem bad, but the good could be bigger. A direct vote might let candidates bribe voters with promises. Also, figuring out ties and keeping things fair in a direct vote system needs thought. So, ditching the Electoral College might not work, but fixing it is a must.

Right now, electors have too much power in deciding the election. The winner-takes-all thing in the Electoral College hurts voters because candidates only focus on "swing" states. Like in 2000, they ignored seventeen states! Because of these issues, the Electoral College is old and needs to be changed or replaced.

Candidates pick electors during their campaigns. These electors help them get votes without visiting every state. Basically, the president just waits for the results. They could even win without much campaigning, because the electors decide. This means candidates could try to bribe electors in key states to win their vote.

Voters actually vote for electors, not the candidates. Electors are picked in different ways, like state meetings or by the candidates. A big worry with the Electoral College is the "disaster factor." This means bad things could happen, like in 1960 when people in Louisiana almost replaced Democratic electors with those against John F. Kennedy.

So, the Electoral College is an old way of doing elections. It gives power to electors instead of the people. Back when people weren't as educated, having representatives pick the president made sense, but it doesn't fit our democracy now.

Listen up, people! Cars? Yeah, they're everywhere. Super useful, sure, but what if we used them less? Think about it – cleaner air and way more cash in your pockets. Seriously, ditching the car a bit more is a smart move.

Take Paris, for instance. Robert Duffer wrote about it: "Paris bans driving due to smog." They got clever. Odd license plates one day, even the next. Boom! If you messed up, you paid. Plus, free public transport for a week! Traffic and smog? Gone! The air got cleaner, and people saved money getting around. Duffer said congestion dropped 60%! "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France, after five days of intensifying smog...rivaled Beijing, China, which is known as one of the most polluted cities in the world". The smog was terrible, but less cars made a HUGE difference.

Then there's Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all-in, basically ditching cars completely. Around 70% don't own one, since it's expensive to park outside the town and it's basically banned there anyway. They LOVE it. Heidrun Walter said "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way". Fewer young people even bother getting licenses these days! As Rosenthal said in "The end of car culture," cars are just ways to get from A to B now. Nobody cares about the car itself, just getting around easily.

Bottom line? Cars get you places, but buses, trains, bikes, and carpools do too. Some are even free! Plus, less pollution and traffic? Win-win for everyone's health and the planet.

The Electoral College? Plumer (Source 2) says it's officially unfair, old news, and makes no sense! Lots of folks feel the same – some think it's a disaster waiting to happen, others just see it as broken. Sure, a few argue for keeping it around, like avoiding runoffs or boosting bigger states. But most think those reasons don't cut it. People who hate the Electoral College usually want a simple popular vote – they think it's way better.

One big problem? All votes aren't equal. Electors get more say than you or me! If we just voted directly, every single person's vote would matter. Plus, electors could get swayed or even bribed, which totally messes up the whole democracy thing.

A lot of people think the Electoral College is just plain outdated. Plumer (Source 2) quotes Bob Dole yelling, "Abolish the Electoral College!" Tons of folks agree; they'd rather just vote directly instead of letting a select group decide. Some even joke that a toddler throwing darts would do a better job. Plumer (Source 2) says, "Over 60 percent of voters want a direct election instead of what we have now," and that number's been climbing since 2000.

And let's be real, the Electoral College is just weird. Electors pick the president, not us directly! It's super confusing. Plumer (Source 2) breaks it down: "You don't vote for the president; you vote for electors who vote for the president. Who are these electors? Basically, anyone who doesn't hold public office. Who picks them? It depends on the state – maybe a party committee, maybe the candidate themselves. Can voters control who their electors vote for? Not always! Do voters screw up and vote for the wrong electors? Sometimes!" It's a mess that makes you wonder who's really in charge.

Okay, maybe the Electoral College does *some* good stuff, like keeping things stable. But most people think the downsides are way worse. A simple popular vote just seems fairer. Like Plumer (Source 2) said, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

For ages, the Electoral College has existed, set up by the people who founded this country and put into our Constitution. I think we should keep this system, because it has worked okay so far, and I don't see any good reason to get rid of it. Keeping the Electoral College makes sense because it helps in, like, five important situations.

Now, the Electoral College could be risky if the people chosen to vote don't stick with their party and decide to use their voting power in a weird way. Basically, people vote for a group of electors who then pick the president. These electors could ignore what the people want and vote how they want. Also, a state might send in two different lists of electors, which would be a mess. While a tie would be super unlikely if everyone just voted directly, the Electoral College always has that chance. In some states, candidates don't even bother campaigning much because one party is just way stronger, which is made worse by the "winner-takes-all" thing. If everyone just voted directly, candidates might try harder in every state to get more votes. But, a lot of the arguments against the Electoral College are about weird situations, so it's good to think about the other side too.

Swing states pretty much decide who wins the presidential election, so the voters there tend to know their stuff. They know their votes matter a lot, so they pay attention to the news and what the candidates are doing. Candidates spend a ton of time and money in these states, trying to win them over. Lots of other states are pretty much decided before the election even starts because one party is just too strong. Voters in swing states probably think more carefully about who they want and what they stand for, so you could say they're the best ones to pick the leader of the country.

The Electoral College can stop us from needing extra elections by making things less confusing. If the U.S. used a direct vote system and the results were really close, not having the Electoral College could cause big problems. Like when Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 won even though they only got around 43% of the vote, that shows why this matters.

I get why some voters in states that lean really hard one way or the other might not like the Electoral College. They might not care as much about the campaigns because they don't think their vote will change anything in that state. But, one person can't decide an election alone, and not everyone even voted in 2012. For most people, just saying who they like is more important than deciding the whole election. Learning about the government makes it more likely that your vote will actually mean something.

Esteemed Senator,

I implore you, keep the Electoral College! This setup has 538 folks called electors, and to win the White House, you need over half, like 270. Each state picks its own electors, usually by the candidate's political group. Keeping the Electoral College is super important because it makes sure we get a clear winner and the president speaks for everyone, not just some people.

One big reason to keep the Electoral College is that it almost always gives one candidate a clear win. Even though it's technically possible to tie in the Electoral College, it's super unlikely, even with the 538 electors being an even number. Take the 2012 election: Obama grabbed almost 62% of the electoral votes but won only around 51% of the popular vote. This happens because most states give all their electoral votes to whoever wins the state, so even a small lead can mean a big win in electoral votes. However, this "winner-take-all" thing means candidates mostly focus on states where the race is tight, ignoring states where they probably won't win. Still, the winner almost always gets a bigger share of the Electoral College votes than their share of the overall votes.

Another key reason to stick with the Electoral College is that the president should stand for all of us. The Electoral College forces candidates to try to win votes from all over the place. No single area has enough electoral votes to choose a president alone. So, even if a candidate is super popular in one region, like Rodney in the South, they still need to campaign hard in other states because racking up more votes in their safe states won't get them more electoral votes. A president without support across the country won't be a good leader. People in other regions might feel left out, like their votes don't matter or the president doesn't really represent them.

Basically, keeping the Electoral College is a must because it stops ties and makes sure the president represents every single American.

Heidrun Walter said that she was always stressed when she owned a car and that she is much happier without one. This idea shows that more and more people are moving away from relying on cars. Cars used to be a must-have for getting around and living a normal life. But now, car sales are down, and people aren't driving as much, which shows that there are good things about using cars less. One big thing is that it can help lower the greenhouse effect, cut down on smog and air pollution, and boost different parts of the economy that aren't just about cars and transportation.

One major point is that the greenhouse effect, which is largely caused by car emissions, puts environments, ecosystems, and life as we know it at risk. "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," it says that cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions and about 50% in some parts of the US. If we limit how much we use cars, these emissions could go down, making life better on Earth. This would have positive effects all over the world, helping with problems like global warming and rising sea levels. So, using cars less would be good because it would lower greenhouse gas emissions.

Another crucial problem is smog and air pollution, which hurt both people's health and the environment. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," points out that "After Days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." This ban, which lasted five days, improved the air quality. If we regularly put these kinds of limits in place, we could reduce pollution and smog. It could also encourage people to use other ways of getting around, like public transportation, bikes, and walking. "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" shows how a capital city that limits car use can lead to more bike paths, parks, sports centers, and better sidewalks, as well as rules for rush-hour traffic. So, using cars less can not only cut down on smog and air pollution but also help create a more lively city environment.

Finally, limiting car use can help the economy grow in different ways. According to one source, most of the transportation money in the US budget has gone to highways. If we relied less on cars, we could free up money for other important things, like education, government programs, and community development. Also, limiting car use can help us save resources, allowing people to invest in other parts of their lives, as another source mentions. The fact that fewer people are driving also shows this trend. One source notes that "Demographic shifts in the driving population suggest that the trend may accelerate. There has been a large drop in the percentage of 16 to 39yearolds getting a license." This means we can focus on rebuilding the economy. Clearly, there are economic benefits to using cars less, including more flexibility in government budgets, saving money, and rebuilding the economy.

To sum up, there are many advantages to limiting how much we use cars. These limits can help the economy, lower greenhouse gas emissions, and reduce smog and air pollution in crowded areas.

Deciding on a presidential pick is super important because that person will totally change the country, for better or worse. The electoral college tries to show what each state wants, though people argue about how good it is. I really think it gives a good look at what most people in each state want, so it shows what the whole country generally wants.

Some folks say the electoral college sucks at picking the best president and call it unfair. But, we still get to say who we like! We still have a big say in what happens. Basically, every vote matters, which is way better than not voting at all. We can always make things better, but things will never be perfect. You just can’t make everyone happy. So, I think the electoral college does a good job of adding up the votes.

Even though it's complicated, it's hard to think of a better way to do things. People mostly worry about if the electoral college really shows what we want. But I don't think that's true. When you vote, you're trying to get votes for the person you think should be in charge. Your vote helps make sure the electoral college goes the way you want. This brings the states together because the most votes show what each state wants, which seems pretty good to me.

Some people complain that the electoral college gives bigger states too much power. That's true, but maybe it makes sense. States with more people naturally get more votes. But, each vote is worth the same, so those states don't get a crazy advantage. The electoral college is a pretty fair way to see what the country wants overall, even if some people don't like it, which you'd expect.

In the end, the electoral college does a good job of showing what each state wants by using the most votes to pick the president. It's not perfect, but just letting everyone vote directly would probably cause even more problems. Every system has issues, but the electoral college is a pretty organized way to add up votes over time. Not everyone will love it, but you can't please everyone.

Dear Senator, So, I have been giving some thought to the best way to elect the President and their crew, and guess what? I have landed on an idea. We've got two old ways of doing this: the Electoral College and just counting up all the votes. After a lot of thinking, I gotta say, one is way better than the other, if you ask me.

Okay, so the Electoral College is where these electors get together and vote for the President and Vice President. These folks, all 538 of them, are the ones who really decide who wins. Honestly, I think this is a mess. We think we're voting for the president, but really, we're just picking these electors who then make the real decision. Why should just a few people, out of millions, get to pick the president while we all just sit around and wait? It just doesn't seem fair that only their opinions really count when we're choosing someone who's gonna run the whole country.

Now, voting by popular vote, that's where everyone gets to vote directly for the President and the team they picked. It means we all get to say who we want to lead and, hopefully, make things better. Even if your pick doesn't win, at least your vote gets counted and shows what you want for the country. Sure, the Electoral College might give you a quicker answer, but knowing your vote matters could get more people involved and thinking hard about who they're voting for. It lets us say what we want, instead of hoping one elector agrees with us.

Okay, I'll admit, the Electoral College isn't all bad. It can make sure the election has a clear winner. Some might say picking electors is basically the same as voting directly. But honestly, these small perks don't even come close to how important it is for everyone to get a direct vote.

So, when it comes down to picking the president, you really gotta ask yourself: How do you want your voice to be heard? Do you want to vote yourself, or let someone else do it for you? For me, it's a no-brainer. I want the system that lets me yell the loudest in our democracy.

Everyone wants to take a relaxing walk in a park, maybe with their kids or grandkids. But, it's getting harder to do this because of all the pollution. We use our cars way too much instead of taking the bus, walking, or carpooling. Cutting back on driving is super important because cars are expensive, stop us from walking or using public transportation, and make the air dirty.

Owning a car costs way more than you think at first. In Vauban, Germany, parking is a pain. You have to buy a pricey parking spot, like maybe forty grand, on top of buying your house. Bogota, Columbia, will fine you twenty-five bucks if you drive on their car-free day. That doesn't sound like a lot, but it adds up. In Paris, if your license plate ends in an even number, you get a twenty-two euro fine on some days. Then, the next day, the odd numbers get fined. They caught almost four thousand drivers! And then you have to pay for gas all the time. Rich people might not care, but it's a big deal for regular families with lots of kids.

We don't always appreciate how good public transportation is. Just imagine if there were no buses, trains, or subways. Try driving into New York City then! In Bogota, on car-free day, people jump on the bus or walk to avoid the fine. The mayor, Antanas Mockus, said that even when it rained, lots of people still joined in. Other cities like Cali and Valledupar copied Bogota, and people from other countries came to watch. New York has a bike-sharing thing so you can get around easy. Even if it's not the coolest thing, public transportation can save the planet.

You can't see the stars at night because of the smog. Paris was super polluted, so they stopped people from driving for a bit to clean up the air. It only got sixty percent better in five days because France likes diesel fuel, which makes pollution. Bogota's car-free day gives you a break from the smog, and Vauban barely has any smog because you can't drive your car near your house. Even small changes can help the environment, even though it's tough.

Vauban and Bogota show us that less driving means less pollution. Paris has to ban cars to get rid of the smog. Parking is expensive in Vauban, and you get fined in Bogota if you drive on the wrong day. This makes people walk or take the bus. Cutting back on driving is important because it costs money, makes us use other ways to get around, and cleans up the air.

Our town could gain big time by ditching our car addiction. Lots of cities in places like France and Colombia are trying to get people to drive less. People there feel way less stressed when they use other ways to get around. Even studies here show folks are buying fewer cars. Now's the perfect time to copy cool spots like Vauban or Bogota and cut down on our time behind the wheel.

Believe it or not, there's a neighborhood in Germany that's proven you can live without cars! In an article, Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, which is pretty much "car-free." People there mostly use public transport. The article said, "70% of families don't own cars, and many even sold their cars to move there!" One resident said she felt much happier without a car since she wasn't tense. Places all over want to be like them and use fewer cars to help the planet. It looks like more "smart planning" spots will pop up. If lots of us want to drive less, we can make it happen, and our city can become a car-free haven.

Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day to get people to use other ways to get around and stop smog. Visitors can't believe how much changes on that day. Going "car-free" gets people moving more and makes the city look nicer. A news article said that parks and sports spots are everywhere, sidewalks are wide and smooth, and cool shops and restaurants have appeared. It's not just about cleaner air; the city's more fun and pretty too!

So, what if we did what Bogota and Vauban do? Car ownership's already dropping here. Fewer people are buying cars or even learning to drive. One writer said that America's not so into cars anymore. Someone else pointed out that the number of miles people drove peaked a while back and has been falling ever since. We're slowly moving away from cars, and young people are a big part of it. We're using social media and phones more, plus carpooling and taking the bus, so we're driving less. The car industry might have to change, but many think this will help us focus on getting around easier and faster.

Basically, driving less means less pollution, less stress, cooler neighborhoods, more exercise, and saving resources. Cities everywhere are trying to cut back on cars to stop pollution. If we go green and car-free, we'll be part of something big!

Esteemed State Senator, I pen this letter to champion the abolition of the Electoral College, convinced that presidential elections ought to hinge exclusively on the nationwide popular vote. My stance? The Electoral College reeks of injustice and menaces the very bedrock of our U.S. Constitution.

The Electoral College? It guts the democratic spirit our nation's founders birthed. Sure, we citizens get to vote, but the Electoral College can flip our collective choice, a slap in the face to popular will! This system makes you wonder if we're a real democracy. Take the 2000 election: Gore won the popular vote, yet Bush nabbed the presidency via the Electoral College. "The Indefensible Electoral College" screams, "Over 60% of voters want a direct election, not this mess." It's clear: most Americans crave a popular vote system.

Furthermore, I blast the Electoral College for menacing the U.S. Constitution, especially citizens' rights. The deal is: we, the people, elect our president. But when the vote champ loses due to the Electoral College, democracy gets a gut punch. "The Indefensible Electoral College" frets about election weirdness: "Imagine a state sending two sets of electors! It happened in Hawaii in '60. Nixon, the VP, luckily okayed only his opponent's electors." Picture the chaos if that double-slate thing happens again and slips through, giving a candidate a cheap win! The Electoral College? A minefield of trouble.

In short, scrap the Electoral College! It's unfair and a big threat to our U.S. Constitution. Mull over these points as you hammer out your own take on this key issue.

Worldwide, transportation causes a lot of pollution. People don't always see how good it is to use cars less. Cars hurt the environment with gas fumes, and owning them can be super stressful. Before you get a car, think hard! The good things about owning one are not as important as the bad.

Cars, trucks, and buses all use gas, which is bad for the earth. These fuels are hurting our planet, which is where we live. If we keep going like this, car pollution could ruin the ozone layer. Then, we might not have a place to live! We use transportation too much. Even when we could walk or bike, we drive. This makes the environment worse. Paris banned some driving to fight pollution. One source says that after five days of bad smog, traffic went down 60 percent! You could describe transportation in many ways, but "death wish" fits.

Owning a car isn't just about money. It can cost a lot! In Vauban, Germany, one source says you can only own a car if you buy a parking spot. That spot can cost $40,000, on top of the house! Plus, cars need gas, which is pricey. Insurance costs more money too. Driving is risky, so being on the road can be stressful and not fun.

Cars are everywhere now, shaping how we live. But, if fewer people depended so much on cars, it could really help our planet. Because so many people drive, pollution is a big problem; it definitely hurts our air.

Bogota, Colombia, started a "Car Free Day," and it might spread to other places. On that day, tons of Colombians walked, biked, or took the bus, and the city wasn't jammed with traffic like it usually is. Even though it rained a bit, lots of folks joined in. The mayor even said the rain didn't stop people. The main point is to get people trying different ways of getting around and make the air cleaner. But, in the U.S., something like this might not work so well because cars are such a big part of life.

Lots of places are worried about dirty air from cars. Paris, for instance, stopped some people from driving to fight really bad pollution. At first, people weren't happy, and almost 4,000 people got tickets, and 27 cars were taken away, says Reuters. But it did cut down on traffic by a lot – like 60 percent! Still, even with less traffic, the smog was like in Beijing, China, which has tons of people. It's still up in the air if folks will really get behind these kinds of rules.

Some people don't want to limit cars or ban driving; they think it could make people angry, especially when it comes to getting around. But, recent studies show that fewer Americans are buying cars, driving, or even getting licenses. A professor at Drexel University thinks that maybe things are changing for good. People who stopped driving during hard times might not even want to go back to it. Car use is slowly going down in the U.S., which is helping to make the air a bit cleaner.

Because we use cars so much, it really messes with the Earth's pollution levels. It's not always easy to limit cars, and there could be some downsides. But, there are good things that can come out of it too. Imagine a world where the air is cleaner, and people bike, skate, or walk more often. Cutting down on cars could make life better and help the planet stay healthy. Less driving could be a big step toward a healthier Earth.

The upside of driving less hinges greatly on where you live and how much you try. Around the world, rules are popping up to check out what happens when towns barely use cars, or ditch them completely. Places choked by smog often have to slash car use, sometimes by tons, to help clean up the air.

Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," really nails what it's like to live car-free. She points out how everything's set up so you can easily walk or bike to get where you need to go. Shifting gears like this is key all over. With pollution so bad, it's a cool test run that we could, fingers crossed, spread globally.

Certain cities are getting hit with scary levels of pollution. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," chats about the crazy smog over Paris, which led to a ban that yanked a bunch of cars off the road. People who broke the rules got slapped with fines, and tons of cars got towed because folks were ticked off about the penalties. Duffer mentions that "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France , after five days of intensifying smog," showing that the ban actually worked to cut down on traffic.

Loads of programs worldwide have nudged millions to hop on pollution-free rides. Columbia's a great example, as Andrew Selsky points out in "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Selsky says that millions of Columbians have switched to walking, biking, or skating instead of driving. Even when it rained, they found ways to get around without cars. Selsky adds that this switch is building a friendlier vibe in Bogota: " Parks and sports centers have also bloomed throughout the city uneven, pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks." It’s a win-win for the planet and the people, and everyone seems to be on board.

As the world changes fast with new kids and gadgets, ditching old habits is a must. Young people are starting to wonder if they really need a car, since there are cheaper, greener options out there. The idea that everyone needs their own car is fading. It might not disappear for good, but it's definitely not as big as it used to be.

The Electoral College is still a hot topic, and many are wondering if it should stay or go. The Electoral College just doesn't seem like a good way for people to vote. Why have a system where we vote for someone who then votes for us? If the United States really lets its citizens pick the president, why doesn't the popular vote decide who wins?

When you vote for a presidential candidate, you're really picking people who will then vote. The article "Does the Electoral College Work?" says that "...when you vote for your candidate you are actually voting for you candidate's electors." The Constitution says we can vote for our president, so how does this system make sense? It doesn't seem right for America and the voting system we should have. To make things worse, can we even trust the electors? As "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" says, "...Can voters control whom their electors vote for? Not always." So, the candidate you like might not get your vote after all. The Electoral College might stop you from directly voting for your president and take away your American right to have your vote actually matter. So, your voice should be heard.

Some say the Electoral College makes sure we have a president, but it doesn't make sure that most people actually want that person. We don't always get the president we voted for. The popular vote often feels pointless, like our voices don't matter to the government. It's like a right we had as Americans is taken away. The popular vote should replace the Electoral College. There have been times when the popular vote didn't match the outcome. The article "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong" talks about an election where Al Gore lost even though he won the popular vote, saying "...according to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency..." This shows how the Electoral College makes individual votes less important when picking the president. The same article says that "...over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now." If our votes really mattered, we should vote on whether to keep the Electoral College or get rid of this bad system.

Some people think the Electoral College keeps things in order and stops big problems in the voting system, but that's not really true. Every system has its problems, but the Electoral College isn't the answer. The United States was founded on the idea of listening to the people's vote, and as an American, I want that to happen and my voice to be heard. I want to vote directly for the candidate I like, not vote for someone else to vote for me.

In short, the Electoral College is unfair, wrong, and unamerican. It stops us from voting for the candidate we want and makes us vote for electors instead. If you believe in American values, you'll see why this system is bad. The Electoral College is not a good way to handle our votes; instead, the people of America should decide elections completely.

Dear Senator,

The need for the Electoral College? People ask this, claiming it ruins real democracy. They say we vote for electors, not who we truly want. But, it works! It makes sure we know who wins and helps make a president for everyone.

It gives us sure winners, which is why it's still around. Just voting by popular vote? That might mess things up more! Richard A. Posner says the winner almost always has way more Electoral College votes than popular votes. This makes it less likely we'll fight over the results. He's right, those Electoral College votes are usually bigger, making things simpler. Plus, it stops ties, like if Nixon and Clinton tied back in '92. Basically, the Electoral College gives us a clearer winner since it often has bigger vote numbers.

Also, it makes the president think about all of us. Candidates need votes from everywhere, not just where people like them. We don't want a president only caring about one area, forgetting the rest! Say Republicans love the South. If a Republican only thinks about the South, that's not fair to other places. Bradford Plumer says it's bad because candidates don't try in states they'll lose. But, maybe those states already decided! Why should a Democrat try hard in Texas if it's Republican? Or a Republican in California if it's Democrat? Voters probably made up their minds! And, old arguments, like Plumer's, might not matter anymore. Things change! To really argue against the Electoral College, you need new facts. See how it worked in recent elections! In 2004, 2008, and 2012, what people thought about it depended on what was happening then, promises, and even if young people voted. So, the Electoral College makes sure the president cares about the whole country.

So, keep the Electoral College! It makes sure we know who wins and helps the president represent everyone. Electoral College votes are usually higher, so we know the results. Plus, it makes the president think about the entire country, not just some areas. Senator, keep it! It's worked well recently, so it'll probably keep working in the future.

Once upon a time, people walked everywhere - to shop, get food, and hang out with family. So why are we all so obsessed with cars now? That's why places all over are trying to make us use cars less.

Ever since cars were invented, they've been a problem for the environment. Now that we know more, they're making rules to cut down on or stop car use. In Vauban, Germany, people live without cars to help the planet, and they mostly love it. As Heidrun Walter, a mom, said, "When I had a car I was always stressed. Now I'm way happier."

Paris, you know, "The City of Love," said no to cars because of bad air pollution. Guess what? Traffic jams went down by 60 percent in only five days!

Paris had the worst air in Europe, so they had to do something to stop the city from being choked by smog. The ban totally helped clean the air and get rid of the smog.

Big cities like New York, with their skinny streets, get super jammed up with lots of cars. Since buildings are so close together, walking or biking can be faster than driving. New York's bike program is proof. Also, carpooling is getting popular nationwide, where people ride together to cut down on cars. That means less pollution, less traffic, and saving money on gas.

Bill Ford, the big boss at Ford, thinks someday owning a car will be a pain. He wants to team up with phone companies to build cities, or a bunch of cities, where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to save time, save energy, cut pollution, and make things safer." We can't just ban cars everywhere since they're easier than trains or walking, but we have to use them less. Too many cars could ruin the planet with pollution. Maybe we could have a day without cars everywhere, and try other ways to get around. For instance, biking is great exercise and faster than walking, so you get healthy and get where you're going!

Presidential elections are still deeply divided due to the Electoral College. Some see it as flawed, while others defend it as working properly. It is a mix of direct vote and congressional election. It has a history of problematic elections, and future issues could be worse. Many ask why Americans don't have total control to directly elect their president, given our focus on democracy. We need a better system, and the Electoral College should go.

One big issue: we technically pick electors who then pick the president. Even if the desired person wins, there are still some problems. For example, the "winner-take-all" rule. Voters aren't treated fairly because of how the system works, according to "The Indefensible Electoral College." Candidates skip states they can't win and focus on tight races in "swing" states. They prioritize some states and ignore others. In 2000, candidates didn't even visit 17 states, which shows how unfair the Electoral College is.

"The disaster factor" is another issue. Bradford Plumer said we got lucky in 2000, and the system could have worse problems. In Louisiana, legislators almost replaced Democratic candidates with those against John F. Kennedy to change the vote. This shows how corrupt the system could be if they got away with it. Other issues could include states sending different electors or a tie. While there are solutions, these possibilities highlight how we need a comprehensive system.

Richard A. Posner argues to keep the Electoral College, saying it balances power. He says smaller states get more votes to balance out larger ones. But this doesn't balance things out, and instead gives smaller states an advantage. Posner also thinks voters in swing states are more involved. Plumer refutes this, noting that many states were ignored by campaigns, including Rhode Island and South Carolina. Voters in many big media markets didn't see any ads. The Electoral College is easily exploited and should be replaced.

I still think the Electoral College needs to be removed. People should have control over electing the president. Even if that's hard, the current system is clearly corrupt. Some say the Electoral College works fine, but the evidence doesn't agree. It needs a total replacement or significant change.

Dear Florida State Senator, Some may say the Electoral College is a fair way to pick the president. Yet, many see it as not fair and too broken to keep using. To fix what's wrong with it, we need a better system. A country calling itself a democracy should have a voting way that shows what democracy means.

Those who like the Electoral College might say ties are rare or that electors will do what voters want. But, just because something is unlikely doesn't mean we can ignore its problems. Like a small rip, the system's flaws can grow. If we don't fix or change the Electoral College, we could face problems too big to solve now. Electors might listen to the people, but sometimes they pick what they want instead.

The Electoral College weakens the people's power a lot. You can vote for who you want, but the elector really chooses our president. Because some states have more electors per person, it feels unfair that some votes matter more than others. This can make people not care about voting. They might think their vote doesn't change anything or make sure their person wins. Also, candidates often ignore states they think they can't win. They focus on big states with more electoral votes. This makes people feel like they don't control the election. This can make fewer people vote because they think it doesn't matter.

Besides less power for people, some think the system isn't as good as others. Even though it doesn't happen often, an electoral tie is possible. With an even number of votes, the House of Representatives would decide. Their choice might not be what the people want. Since Congress is usually on one side or the other, letting them pick the president could seem unfair. Also, someone can win the popular vote but lose the election because of the Electoral College. This happened with Al Gore in 2000. This system has too many problems to be really good. We must fix these before using the Electoral College to pick presidents. If we don't, more problems could happen.

The Electoral College might work, but it's not good and doesn't care enough about the people. The electors, not us, choose the president. In a country that says people have power and control, we don't have much say. We need a better system that shows what democracy really means. We need to speak out against the overrated Electoral College and make a system that fits what America should be.

Loads of folks all over use cars because they're usually an easy way to travel. But, all that exhaust hurts the environment a lot. Good thing there are ways to drive less. This is super helpful! Think: fewer greenhouse gases, less smog, and keeping our resources safe.

First off, less driving means way less greenhouse gas in the air. Switching to bikes, trains, or just walking? Easy! Vehicle exhaust makes greenhouse gases, and those gases are bad news. Almost half of those gases come from regular cars, says source 1. So, fewer cars equals a healthier planet.

Plus, using cars less helps cut down on smog. Smog is nasty, and it messes with the environment and our health. It's polluted air that can make you sick. Lots of cities have worse smog because of too many cars. Like, Paris almost had worse air than China, which is super polluted, according to source 2. Drive less, and we all breathe easier. Check this out: Paris cut car use for just five days, and smog dropped sixty percent! Cleaner air is a big win when we ditch the cars.

Finally, less driving is good for saving resources. Source 1 says we could build smaller cities if we didn't rely on cars so much. This means less land gets used for highways, malls, and houses. That would save tons of materials for building, plus the natural resources themselves, says source 4. Basically, driving less would make life better for everyone.

In short, if we didn't rely so much on cars, our planet would be happier, we'd be healthier, and we'd save a bunch of stuff we need.

Even with many calls to get rid of it, the Electoral College actually has more good points than bad. The Constitution put it there on purpose. It was a smart way to split the power between Congress picking the President and people voting directly. This way, everyone in the country gets a say, and the leaders of each state can really fight for what their people want. People complain about it, sure, but the Electoral College is still a pretty good way to pick the President.

Presidential hopefuls have to try to get everyone in the country to like them because of how the Electoral College works. Like, someone who's only popular in the South can't win, because that area alone doesn't have enough electoral votes to make them president. So, they might not have to campaign much where they're already popular, but they'll need to work hard to win over people in places where they're not as well-liked. This is good because we want a president that most people agree on. This also makes them more likely to do well in office. Candidates spend more time and money on swing states, and less on states where they know who will win. As a result, voters in those swing states are more involved and know more because they know they're important for deciding the election. These voters are usually the most thoughtful and involved, so they're perfect for helping decide who wins.

Also, the Electoral College should stay because it usually does what the people want. Okay, so Al Gore won the popular vote in 2000, but George W. Bush won the election through the Electoral College. That doesn't happen often. It hasn't happened since 1888. Parties pick people to be electors, and those people are supposed to vote for the party's candidate. They almost always do. People want to switch to a popular vote system, but that might cause more problems than the Electoral College does. Plus, it's possible to have a tie in the popular vote, since there are an even number of total votes. But that's super unlikely. It's never happened. The Electoral College also solves the problem of what happens when no one gets a majority of the votes.

Some say the Electoral College makes people not want to vote because they think their candidate doesn't have a chance in their state, like Republicans in California. But every vote helps decide how many electoral votes a state gets. And people vote in presidential elections because they want to show who they support, not because they think their one vote will decide everything. The Electoral College has worked well for over 100 years, and it will keep working if we stick with it.

Helping our planet thrive? Easy – cut back on driving! Get this: a study in Europe showed cars are behind a whopping 12% of greenhouse gases. Sure, ditching the car seems tough. But hey, tons of options exist! Carpool, bum a ride with family, hop on a bus or train, grab a taxi, or even better – bike! So, it's not about a lack of choices, more like a lack of will to save nature.

Cars bad for the environment? Obvi! Cities are cracking down with taxes and penalties to curb car use. Take Paris, for example. After choking on smog, they temporarily banned driving! Proof that the world isn't worse than it could be! Beijing used to rival Paris in pollution until authorities stepped in. Bogota is another example; they repeatedly ban over 7.2 million residents from driving on "Day Without Cars." There are consequences for non-compliance, with potential fines around $25 and even arrests. Andrew Selsky described the event as a "program set to spread to other countries, millions of Colombians hiked, biked, skated, or took buses to work during this car free day". It’s not just about cleaner air, it's about people hanging out together in parks and stuff.

Fewer cars equal a healthier you, no joke! Less traffic means cleaner air. More walking equals less heart disease, obesity, you name it! Car companies might not love it. Ford, Toyota, Honda and friends could see profits tank if we buy fewer cars, though most people won’t ditch their cars completely. But, a drop in car making could boost the economy by saving the government cash on fuels. Here's the kicker: people are driving less each year! Sociologists are guessing that this could be awesome for the planet, even if car companies aren't thrilled.

Guess what? Car use is going down, down, down! Young people aren't buying cars. A 2013 study found a 23% drop in driving among young people from 2001 to 2009. Experts are staying chill, but the drop in sales has some folks thinking the U.S. has hit "peak driving". Who knows what'll happen? Good stuff? Bad stuff? Too soon to tell. Obama’s goals to cut greenhouse gases seem to have worked since studies now show that "Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by".

Senator, this Electoral College—it's pretty controversial, right? We gotta decide: keep it like it is, or switch to a system where the person with the most votes wins, plain and simple. I really think we should push for that popular vote thing. Why? Well, not everyone even bothers to vote now. Maybe half of us do, if that. And maybe that's 'cause folks think their vote doesn't matter much in states where everyone knows who's gonna win, like California or Texas. Check out "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23 for more on that. Plus, the way it is now, candidates just focus on those "swing states," ignoring everyone else.

Another big reason to think about changing things, Senator, is this: "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," says "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. I think people don't bother because they don't see any point if their candidate doesn't have a shot in their state. And get this: some folks even tried to mess with the Electoral College in the past. Like those segregationists who tried to ditch Democratic Electors to hurt Kennedy's chances—read about it in "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11—shows how fragile this system can be.

Now, I get it, Senator. There are arguments for keeping the Electoral College. Some say it's a compromise the Founding Fathers came up with to make sure everyone gets a fair say. It gives "qualified citizens in congress" a deciding voice, as "What is the Electoral College" Section 1 says. Maybe the Electoral College is okay as the populace still has a role in influencing the electors' choices, with historical precedent being a significant aspect, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15. Ideally, electors are good people who'll vote for whoever won the most votes, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16. But even if the electoral votes are counted right, the popular vote can still be higher, though it doesn't happen often—"In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 mentions that too.

Senator, this is complicated, but we gotta talk about how unfair the winner-take-all thing is. Candidates spend all their time and money in the big states, running tons of ads, and forget about the smaller ones that they think they've already won. And those "swing states" get even more attention. Get this: "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," from "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13. That shows you how much they care about those big states and "swing states." It's gotta be discouraging for voters in the states everyone ignores.

So, Senator, I really think we need to switch to a popular vote system. It would make people want to vote, knowing they can actually change things and pick who leads their state and country. We should always remember: "Power to the People." That's what matters here.

Cars? They're just *everywhere* these days. You hardly hear about walking or biking, do you? We're all stuck in our cars, and that changes everything. It feels normal, but maybe it shouldn't. Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky? They get it. Each has their own reasons for wanting fewer cars, but they all agree: we need less of them. After reading their articles, it's obvious: less car use saves time, helps businesses, saves resources, and weakens the power of "car culture."

Late for work? Ugh, that morning traffic. Walking or biking (if you can!) could be faster. Selsky said Colombia had a car-free day where "millions... hiked, biked, skated or took buses," making the streets "eerily... devoid of traffic jams". Less traffic means less waiting! Walking can save time and help the Earth. Businesses do better with fewer cars too. Selsky noted "new restaurants and upscale shopping districts" popping up, and old sidewalks replaced with "broad, smooth sidewalks". People walk more, see more stores, and spend more money!

Saving resources? HUGE reason to ditch the car. Rosenthal says cars make up "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-intensive areas". Yikes! We *have* to drive less. Car fumes are bad news for the planet. Some cities even try "no-driving" days. Duffer mentions Paris having a "partial driving ban" after "days of near-record pollution". Less pollution, less traffic, less smog – that's what happens when we drive less! Amazing.

Car culture is fading, maybe. Studies show young Americans drive less. Rosenthal mentions a "23 percent" decrease in driving by young people. Maybe it's tickets, parents, or caring about the environment, who knows? But Rosenthal also said that experts believe these trends will benefit the "carbon emissions and the environment". Car companies are changing because of this, and the changing of commute times due to technology.

So, less driving saves time and helps businesses, saves resources, and weakens car culture. Next time you travel, think about driving less. It's better for the planet.

The Electoral College is important, and here's why it should stay. Even though some say it's old, it's still the fairest way to pick our President. First off, it makes election results less likely to cause big arguments than if we just counted up all the individual votes. Plus, it means we don't need to hold a second round of voting. Lastly, it stops bigger states from having too much power in politics. Keeping the Electoral College around is key to avoiding problems.

Electoral College wins are less likely to lead to fights than a simple count of all votes. Often, a candidate gets way more electoral votes than their share of the popular vote would suggest. Think about Obama: he got almost 62% of the electoral vote, even though only about 51% of people voted for him. Because of the "winner-take-all" rule in most states, it's easy to see who wins each state's electoral votes when someone gets the most votes, even if it's not a majority. Even though a tie in the Electoral College is possible, it's super unlikely, even with an even number of electoral votes total. Just counting the popular vote would probably cause way more trouble.

The Electoral College stops us from needing RunOff elections. These happen when no one gets more than half the votes. For instance, "Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 both had only 43 percent plurality of the popular votes, while winning a majority in the Electoral College 301 and 370 Electoral Votes". The Electoral College makes it clear who the winner is, so we don't have the headache of needing a second vote. Because we have the Electoral College, we don't need to worry about RunOffs.

To wrap it up, the Electoral College does a good job of keeping big states from having too much say. I really think we should stick with the Electoral College like we always have. Even if there might be better ways to do things in theory, the Electoral College is the best system we have right now.

My mom’s iPhone camera flashes as I grin. The small, plastic rectangle in my hand, maybe four by three inches, feels like freedom. This is the kid dream: belly down in the kitchen, a toy car underfoot as Mom cooks, garlic strong in the air. I finally have my driver's license! For teenagers, it's a big deal, but many forget the downsides of driving. Depending less on cars offers perks like cleaner air and better health for everyone.

Now that I have my license, I hate waking up to thick smog and squinting on my way to school. But, my car is part of the problem. Car exhaust causes smog and air pollution from greenhouse gas emissions. In the U.S., cars make up about 50% of these emissions. Source 1. A lot more comes from making gasoline and diesel, which wastes energy and releases carbon. Making car fuel releases greenhouse gases, and then the cars release even more! In AP Environmental Science, I know that too many greenhouse gases are bad. These gases trap sunlight, even UV rays, and the planet heats up. This is global warming. Plus, air pollution looks awful. Paris had so much smog that they banned some cars to clean the air. Cutting the number of cars in half for one day cleared the smog, so people could go back to normal. Source 2. Smog hurts Paris' economy because tourists want to see Versailles, not get stung by pollution.

Cutting back on cars helps people's bodies and minds. Vauban, a town in Germany, got rid of cars completely. 57% of residents sold a car to move there, and they are happier. Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Source 1. Walker's words show how not using cars improves mental health. Physical health gets better too. People walk, jog, bike, or skate to get around. In Bogota, Colombia, they have a "Day Without Cars" every year, so people walk around the big city. Source 3. Walking is good for your heart. If more places had "Day Without Cars," maybe fewer people would have clogged arteries.

In short, using cars less has many good points, like less air pollution and improved physical and mental health. Next time you grab your keys to drive a short distance to a friend's house, think about what you're doing. Think about your health and the planet's health, and try walking.

Voting matters a lot; it only happens sometimes. When we vote, we get to say what we want and help decide where our country goes. It's best if we just pick the president by who gets the most votes. That way, we all get to choose directly. We should pick who we like, and nobody should stop us. The popular vote is fairer and easier than the Electoral College. The Electoral College feels more complicated.

It's often said that the popular vote is more fair than the Electoral College because it's simple: everyone votes, and whoever has the most wins. Candidates usually try to visit many states to campaign, if they can, depending on how much time they have. However, Bradford Plumer said that with the Electoral College, "candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning, focusing only on the tight races in the "swing" states." This doesn't seem right because every state should get to see and hear from the people who want to be president. They should understand what these candidates want to do for the country. Sometimes, candidates totally ignore whole states. Plumer points out that "during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't get to see a single campaign ad." If people don't see the candidates and hear what they think, how can they know who to vote for? It's hard to decide when you don't know who is who.

Also, the popular vote is less confusing than the Electoral College. It can cause problems. With the popular vote, it's easy. You vote, they count the votes, and the person with the most becomes president. The Electoral College? Well, Plumer says that if there's a tie, "the election would be thrown to the House of Representatives, where state delegations vote on the president. The Senate would choose the vice president. Because each state casts only one vote, the single from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." Can you imagine such a mess, where one vote counts for so many different people? The popular vote keeps things simple. Everyone gets a vote, and most votes win. No need for representatives to step in and potentially misrepresent us.

In short, the popular vote is better because it's fairer. We get to pick who we want without anyone else interfering. Plus, it's way less of a headache than the Electoral College. A tie could mean representatives vote, and they might not even know what all their people want. Voting is serious, and we need to treat it that way. We should all get to vote for who we like, and no one else should make that decision for us, not even those representatives. Voting really, really matters.

The Electoral College is a pretty simple way to vote that works. The Office of the Federal Register explains how it works in "What Is the Electoral College." First, people pick electors. Then, these electors get together and vote for who should be President and Vice President. After that, Congress counts up all the votes. So, basically, we pick people who then vote for the President. It seems to work okay, right? So, why mess with it?

Lots of folks want to get rid of the Electoral College and just use the popular vote. Bradford Plumer asks in his article, "Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong," "Can voters really trust their electors to vote the way they want?" He says, "Not really." Okay, maybe that's true sometimes, but we still get to pick the electors we think will choose the right person for president. We gotta trust them. Even if we voted directly for president, we'd still have to trust that they'll do a good job. It's pretty much the same idea, just with an extra step.

Richard A. Posner makes a good point in his article, "Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President." He says the Electoral College helps even things out because the Senate already favors smaller states. It's true! Big states get less power in the Senate because of this. So, if we just went by popular vote, those big states would get even MORE attention during elections because they have more people.

Plus, the Electoral College can stop things from getting messy if no one gets over half the votes. Take 1968, for instance. Nixon got more votes than anyone else (43 percent), but he didn't have a clear majority. Clinton was in the same boat in 1992. But both of them won the Electoral College, so we didn't have to have another election to decide who won.

I think we should stick with the Electoral College. It works well and stops states with lots of people, like Texas, from having too much power compared to smaller states, like Rhode Island. It also helps us avoid runoffs. Some people might think it's old-fashioned, but I think the Electoral College is still a good way to vote.

It's just plain obvious: the electoral college? Totally unfair, super old, and makes no sense. People say it's got good points, but that's mostly just talk. And the reasons against direct voting? Weak! Even though it might sting, Bob Dole had it right: ditch the electoral college. The main problem? Even the smartest defenses of this thing are just wrong. Lots of folks who like the electoral college don't get that electors can't always be trusted. Just 'cause you vote for an elector who says they'll vote for a certain president, doesn't mean they actually will. Think about it: People like Nixon, Carter, Dole, even the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and the AFL-CIO, all wanted to get rid of the electoral college at some point. Everyone basically wants a system where the person with the most votes wins. Right now, we're not really voting for who we want as president; we're voting for these electors. Plus, if it's a tie, the House of Representatives decides. And that's a group where some people's votes count way more than others!

So, voting for electors isn't the same as voting for the president. Can you be sure those electors will do what they said? Nope. Do people sometimes mess up and vote for the wrong electors? Yep. This whole "electoral college" thing is messed up and doesn't work. People keep thinking they're voting straight for the president, but that's not always true. The big thing is, we don't pick the electors. So, who says they'll be loyal and do what the people want? Like in 1960, some people in Louisiana almost switched out the Democratic electors for ones who hated Kennedy. That would've totally messed up the vote. It's easy to lie and cheat with this system. The electoral college is a shaky thing to build our country on. "Faithless" electors have even gone against their party and voted for someone else. That could happen again, and it's just not honest. Can't trust the electoral college. People should get to vote directly.

Also, if the electoral vote is tied, the House picks the president and vice president. But how they do it is just as bad as the electoral college. It's all unfair and not democratic. Each state gets one vote. So, the rep from Wyoming, with 500,000 voters, has as much power as the 55 reps from California, with 35 million voters. Is that smart? No way! That's how things work on this planet. Lots of people vote for one party for president and another for Congress. So, the House's choice might not be what the people actually want. And if you think a tie is rare, think again: In 1968, just 41,971 votes could've caused a tie. In 1976, only 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 in Hawaii would've done it. A few votes can mess everything up. People always say "your vote matters," but is that really true with all this going on? It's crazy the Electoral College is still up and running. Most people want to switch to a simple popular vote.

To sum it up, dumping the electoral college and just voting for president would be way better. Voting for electors isn't the same as voting for who you want, and if it's a tie, the House doesn't represent everyone fairly. People should get to vote fairly and not be tricked into voting the wrong way. Let's hear the people! "Let us Vote!" A president who says they're "For the people" should be picked "By the people."

Here are some thoughts and details on how using cars too much can cause big problems. Cutting back on cars is super helpful, not just for the planet, but also for cities and people like you and me. It’s not just America that needs to use fewer cars; places like Central America and Europe have this problem too. So, it's not a small thing; it's something the whole world is dealing with. We all know the planet is getting warmer, and a lot of that comes from burning gas, especially from cars. So, why is driving less so important, anyway?

Okay, so in the article "Paris bans driving due to smog," paragraph 10 starts with, "After days of near-record pollution..." What does that even tell us about how we live now?
