Paris is almost breaking records for how polluted it is, and that's seriously bad news! If Paris, the super famous "City of Love," is dealing with this, what about other cities around the world? Cities keep getting bigger, factories pop up everywhere, and everyone's buying cars. How are we ever going to drive less? All this city growth is just going to make the pollution problem even worse! In a few years, these problems we're ignoring now are going to be huge. And if things are bad now, imagine how much worse they'll be later! We totally need to pay attention to this.

David Goldberg mentions in paragraph seven of "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," that "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Since when did cars become more important than staying healthy? What makes a car so special? Sure, they get us places faster, but what are we giving up for that? It's not just about car pollution; it's also about how we're living. Parks are disappearing because tax money is going to roads. We're seeing malls and fast food on all the highways, so we only see nature out the car window. We need trees for air, but if pollution is killing them, when are we going to realize it's too late to fix it? Before, no one cared about using cars too much, because it wasn't a big deal. But now, everyone's talking about pollution.

Even though we have to cut back on cars, there's still hope for cleaning things up. We can start small, like having car-free days. And if you drive on those days, you get a ticket, like in Bogota, Colombia. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" talks about that. It looks like it's helping, because Cali and Valledupar, other cities in Colombia, are trying it out, too.

During last summer, I took my first trip to the West Coast in California! I always thought of it as being super diverse, with surfing spots, snowy mountains, and stuff like that. When I got to Los Angeles, I was kind of expecting it to be like a super energetic city like Miami, but I was really surprised. There was a lot of smog and traffic, and that really messed up the beauty of California for me. It's a pretty common thing in cities that rely on cars a lot, and it would be way better if we could decrease the number of cars there. That would make the air cleaner, and it'd be way safer for people walking around, and even for drivers!

In source 1, Elizabeth Rosenthal talks about car-free cities, and she says that "Passenger cars are responsible for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in carintensive areas of the United States" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). But, that doesn't really show the whole problem because delivery and commercial trucks add to it too. More and more people are realizing that greenhouse gasses are super bad for the atmosphere, and that's why there's a bigger push to use fewer cars. Places like France, Colombia, and the United States are doing a lot to make that happen. According to source 4, these efforts seem to be working. Rosenthal says "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by" (paragraph 29). Cars won't just disappear, but using them less can really help save the planet.

Also, making it harder to drive cars in super crowded areas where lots of people walk, like in New York City, would definitely make fewer car accidents happen. In the US, there are about 500 car accidents every day, and probably over 5000 around the world. Car accidents are a big cause of death in a lot of places, which is really sad because a lot of them could be stopped from happening. A lot of times, drivers just get too confident. Also, cell phones and texting have made it worse. But, good news! Programs that try to lower greenhouse gas emissions are also lowering traffic accidents. Duffer says, "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Source 3 also talks about how Bogota's Car Free Day is inspiring other countries in Latin America to do the same thing, so they can have less traffic and be safer for people walking.

Overall, people are starting to see why it's important to use fewer cars. When you use less cars, the air is cleaner, which is good for everyone, and it also helps stop accidents. After my trip to California was over, I got out of the city and off the highway, and I was finally able to see how beautiful the nature really is!

Ever thought about what makes the air dirty? Cars are a huge problem, for one thing. Less driving? Great perks! Think clean air, more cash, and safer streets. Let me explain.

First off, fewer cars mean way less air pollution. Makes sense, right? Like in Paris, when the air was super bad, they stopped some cars from driving. Air got better fast! So, fewer cars equal cleaner air. Plus, less driving cuts down on big oil companies burning stuff, meaning fewer nasty things in the air. Bikes, feet, buses, skateboards, subways? All cleaner than cars!

Another sweet deal? Saving money! Seriously. Get caught driving when you shouldn't (like those "25 fines" people got)? Ouch. Plus, cars eat up cash: gas (getting pricier all the time!), tickets, and fixing stuff (tires, lights, oil). Drive less, save more over time.

Last, but not least: safety! One article said getting all kinds of transport working together – walkers, bikes, cars, trucks, buses – is "to improve safety." Less traffic means fewer crashes and fewer people dying in them. Also, fewer cars are safer for motorcycles, bikes, and people walking. And, less drunk driving, too.

So, bottom line: driving less is awesome. Cleaner air, more money, safer streets. What's your reason for ditching the car sometimes?

Lots of folks think having a car is a must for today's world. Cars are still super common for getting around, but they really mess with the environment and make cities less friendly for walking and public transit. But, using cars less is a great idea! Lots of places around the world show that it can work. If we cut down on cars, our environment, hangout spots, and buses/trains could get way better.

Cars are a big problem for the planet. In an article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," Elisabeth Rosenthal talks about how Vauban, Germany, is trying to cut down on greenhouse gases. The article says that cars cause 12% of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe, and even 50% in some car-heavy spots in the US. These emissions cause big problems, making cities unsafe. If we didn't rely so much on cars, these numbers could drop a lot. Sometimes, the government has to step in to clean up cities because of car pollution. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," talks about how Paris, France, had to ban cars because of bad smog. "After days of really bad pollution, Paris said no driving for some cars to clean the air. Motorists with even-numbered license plates had to stay home or pay a 22euro fine. The same thing happened the next day for odd-numbered plates." This ban worked great! "Traffic went down 60% in Paris after five days of smog!" This shows that even small changes in car use can make things much better.

Less car use also makes cities nicer. Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," talks about how Bogota, Colombia, has gotten better by using fewer cars. "The day without cars is part of a plan that started in Bogota in the mid1990s. They've built 118 miles of bike paths, the most in Latin America. Parks and sports places have popped up everywhere. Bad sidewalks have been replaced with smooth ones. Rush-hour traffic is way down, and new restaurants and shops have opened up." Cities are starting to think about people who don't use cars. They know that cars can be bad for cities, so they are trying to make things better for everyone, not just drivers. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal talks about how the US wants to use cars less. Bill Ford, who is in charge at Ford Motor Company, wants to work with phone companies to make cities where "walking, biking, cars, buses, and trains all work together to save time, use less energy, lower emissions, and be safer." Lots of people are trying to make public places better for people who want to get around in a way that is good for the environment.

People are starting to realize that we don't need cars as much, so they are using them less. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal says that fewer people are getting licenses and buying cars. "America isn't as crazy about cars anymore. If you count how many miles people drive, it peaked in 2005 and has been going down since then, according to Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives." The article also says that people don't need to own a car all the time anymore. "The Internet lets people work from home and talk to friends without driving. Cities are getting nicer, so people are moving back from the suburbs. Phones and carpooling apps make it easier to get around, like shared van services for work. Because of all these things, people who stopped driving because of the recession might not go back to it." Now, it's easier than ever to get a car when you need one. You don't need to use a car all the time.

Using cars less is a good idea. When we have fewer cars on the road, we can fix big problems like pollution. Cities are changing to make public places better for everyone. Cutting down on cars is super important to make things better for lots of people, like their work, time, money, and how good their life is, both in their town and around the world.

I may be young and haven't seen much, but the Electoral College debate has been going on forever. You might not care what a 14-year-old thinks, since I can't even vote for four more years. But, please, listen to me. I really think we should keep the Electoral College. Here's why: A president chosen by popular vote might not be the best leader, and a popular vote could mean a president only wins because one area really likes them.

Basically, if we elect our president by popular vote, we could end up with someone bad for the country. Article three, paragraph 20 says, "The winner-take-all method makes candidates focus on tossup states." These states decide the election, so people there are more informed. But with a popular vote, anyone over 18 can vote for silly reasons. Let's be real, young voters might pick who their friends like or just vote for their party. A lot of people would make dumb choices without thinking hard, which the Electoral College makes us do.

Also, a popular vote might elect a president just because they're popular in one area. Article three, paragraph 19 says a candidate with only regional appeal won't be a good president and no region has enough votes to elect someone alone. Imagine if the popular vote decided things. Say Rodney is super popular in the South. Now, Rodney doesn't waste time campaigning there since it won't get him more electoral votes. But if it was all about popularity, voters in other areas might feel ignored, and the president wouldn't care about them. Even if he won, they'd feel like he wasn't "their president." The Electoral College stops that. We vote for electors who then vote for president. The person with the most votes wins all the electors in that state. This is fair. A popular vote would unfairly hurt those who didn't vote for the winner.

Of course, some people disagree. Article two says, "Candidates don't spend time in states they can't win... In 2000, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all." So, candidates ignore states they think they'll lose. I get how that's an argument for a popular vote, where candidates would have to campaign everywhere. But there are good reasons against that. A candidate would have to visit all 50 states, but that doesn't mean they'd win. The Electoral College makes sure the majority in a state matters, not just overall popularity. It's like high school: the most popular kid isn't always the nicest, and someone less popular might be really kind. Electing a president based on popularity could really hurt our country.

So, Mr. State Senator, I think the Electoral College is really important. A popular vote could mean we get a bad president, or someone who only wins because one area likes them. I really hope you think about my letter when you talk about this.

Driving less? That could mean a world that's both safer and healthier, you know.

Think about it: less car exhaust means cleaner air! Everyone gets to breathe easier.

Sure, ditching the car might bug some folks, but hey, it helps us all out in the long run. Breathing in all that yucky air all the time? Seriously bad for you, especially your lungs. Plus, fewer drives equal fewer trips to the gas station, and less money spent on fixing the car! Extra cash for, well, anything else.

Owning a car? Big bucks! Insurance, gas every week… Even if gas prices dipped a bit lately, they could shoot right back up, leaving you broke.

So, is driving *really* worth what it does to the planet, and your wallet?

Wouldn't life be easier if we didn't lean on cars so much?

It's your call, really.

Ever stop to think about all the junk your car spits out when you drive? Probably not. More likely, you're thinking about getting there, or texting, and *not* the environment. Get it – it's not exactly top of mind. Usually, it takes some awful news report about air quality to even make us notice.

The slow creep of car pollution makes it easy to miss just how bad it is for our world.

Eventually, plants keel over left and right, and people? They're gasping for air, maybe even ending up in the hospital because they can't breathe!

Think about the peace of mind from not worrying about car payments or filling up the tank.

Tons of people and families are always stressed about money, just trying to pay bills and afford enough gas to even get to work.

Instead of a car sucking up gas, what if you just walked or biked to work? Talk about saving some serious dough!

Getting rid of the Electoral College? Bad idea. The Founding Fathers set this up, writing it into the Constitution to mix Congress's power with what the people want. Some say it's not fair now because electors pick the president, not us directly, and voting for a person is really just voting for a group of electors. But, worries about electors going against what we vote for? Rare and usually overblown. So, despite claims of unfairness, keep the Electoral College. It makes sure we have a clear winner, stops the need for runoffs, and gives smaller states more say.

Sure, the Electoral College could be a mess, like in 2000, but that's less likely than fighting over the popular vote count. This means someone could win the electoral vote but lose the popular vote. Take 2012, when Obama grabbed 61.7% of the electoral vote with only 51.3% of the popular vote. The way most states do it, winner-takes-all, can make a win seem huge even if it's close.

Plus, the Electoral College stops elections where no one gets a majority. Think 1968 and 1992, when winners got only 43% of the vote – could've led to ties and runoffs! But the Electoral College picks a clear winner. Some also say it keeps folks from voting in states where their candidate probably won't win, like Texas Democrats or California Republicans. Why vote if it feels pointless?

However, the Electoral College balances things out, giving smaller states a voice against the big ones. In 2012, Florida was close, and its 29 electoral votes went to Obama. Big states get more love from candidates because they matter more in the Electoral College. It is often claimed that the winner-take-all system isn't fair to us, but candidates focus on swing states instead of wasting time where the outcome's obvious.

While some might not vote because their vote feels unimportant, one vote never swings an election. People vote to say what they believe, not because they think they alone will decide who wins. This shows why the Electoral College, not the popular vote, should still pick our leader.

The electoral college? It's old, unfair to voters, and a recipe for major trouble. A national popular vote makes way more sense. Each person gets one vote, period, no matter where they live. The arguments against it? Weak.

The electoral college? It's just not fair. Check out Richard A. Posner's "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President." He talks about Florida in 2012. Obama got all the electoral votes, even though he only won about 62% of the vote. That means the other 48%? Their votes didn't matter at all! Winner takes all, even if it's close. And swing states? They get all the attention, says Bradford Plumer in "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the bestlaid defenses of the system are wrong." In 2000, seventeen states? Basically ignored by the candidates. It all comes down to a few swing states. With a popular vote, everyone gets the same attention. Each vote counts the same, everywhere.

But wait, there's more! The electoral college could cause a real mess. Plumer's article? It talks about electors going rogue, not voting how they're supposed to. Rare, maybe. But one person could mess up the whole thing. And what if there's a tie? Then each state gets one vote. Super unfair! Millions of people in big states? Their vote counts the same as a few in tiny states. A popular vote fixes all of this.

Richard A. Posner defends the electoral college in his article? Those reasons don't hold water! He says popular vote would lead to more disputes? No proof! He says candidates would just stay in their own regions? Maybe true, but those regions don't have enough people to win. He says the electoral college educates voters in swing states? Limited thinking! A popular vote would educate *everyone*. He argues it helps bigger states? Nope. Candidates will focus on big populations anyway. The only maybe valid point is runoff elections. They could be tricky. But it's still better than being unfair or risking disaster!

Bottom line? Defending the electoral college is tough. Too much evidence shows it's unfair and dangerous. A national popular vote? It's a must. Supporting the electoral college? You're backing unfairness and potential chaos. No one wants that!

America's known for promising life, freedom, and the chase of happiness, plus all the free stuff it hands out, and the idea of running things without picking favorites. But, the Electoral College? Still a fight. This thing has folks picked by states voting for president, and whether that's fair? People keep arguing about it. Lots of folks think the Electoral College just isn't fair or trustworthy, and here's why.

Imagine your elector promising to back your guy for president, then switching their vote. Boom! You get a president you hate or don't trust. Voters don't have total power over what their electors do, which many see as messed up. A straight-up popular vote would let people make sure their choice counts in the election, instead of maybe going to someone they didn't pick. It's happened before where someone won the popular vote but lost the election because of the Electoral College. The people's choice didn't get them the White House. We fought hard for our basic rights, and a fair vote is definitely one of them.

Besides messed-up votes, the Electoral College brings other issues to the table. A tie in a presidential election seems crazy, but history shows races have been super close. So, what happens if it's a total tie? Some might say the Electoral College can fix it, but not really. Since the Electoral College has fewer people deciding, a tie could be even more likely. One elector changing their mind could be like thousands of votes switching. Plus, electors often get perks when their candidate wins, even though tons of people in their state, or even the whole country, helped them get there and deserve some credit.

So, the Electoral College makes you wonder about fairness and if you can trust it. Americans can't fully control their electors' votes, and a huge tie is more possible. America's all about being fair and giving everyone a shot, so giving people a fair and direct vote is a must.

What if we thought about what happens if we keep using cars so much? I mean, think about the dirty air, what it does to our sky, and how we're using up stuff we can't replace. We might end up in a world where we need a mask just to breathe because of all the smog! Going "green" and driving less can really help our towns and the Earth. We'd have cleaner air, nicer places to live, and even better ways to get around. Trust me, once you go green, you won't want to go back!

Smog is a big problem when everyone drives all the time, especially in big cities. All the stuff that comes out of cars gets stuck in the air and makes it hard to breathe for a lot of people. Like, Paris, in France, had to say "no cars allowed" for a bit because the air was so bad. They stopped people from driving on certain days, and it actually made the smog better. The air got almost as good as it is in Beijing, China, which is known for being super polluted! But once the smog was gone, they let cars back, and now they're basically back where they started since everyone's still driving.

But check this out: some cool towns are popping up where people don't drive much at all! There's this place called Vauban in Germany that's made to be car-free. Get this: 70% of the people there don't even own a car, and 57% sold their cars just to move there! One of the people who lives there, Heidrum Walter, said, "When I had a car I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." And Bogota, in Colombia, has been trying out ways to get people to drive less too. These car-free lifestyles are showing us that life can be good for us and the planet!

Okay, so cars aren't going away completely, but there are some cool new ways to get around that are catching on. In New York City, a ton of people are using the bike-sharing program. And more people are carpooling to cut down on the number of cars on the road. Studies are even showing that fewer young people in the US are getting driver's licenses and buying cars. All of this means that we could actually live in a world with way fewer cars!

So, to wrap it up, driving less is a great way to make the Earth healthier. It can help us fight smog, make our neighborhoods greener, and come up with better ways to get around!

Mr. Goldberg, from Transportation for America, a group pushing for car-free living, thinks we built everything after WWII around cars, and that's gotta change. The world's moving on, and cars, once so great, are starting to lose their grip. So, why ditch the car keys? Let's dig in.

Places like Germany are seeing this happen faster than expected. Vauban, a town that started in 2006, is trying out "smart planning." Basically, it's a place where cars are becoming useless. More and more folks are walking, biking, or hopping on the tram to get to work or school from the suburbs. "Smart planning" means designing towns so you don't need to drive everywhere. A whopping 70% of families in Vauban don't own a car and love not stressing about driving every day. Get this: 57% sold their cars just to move to Vauban! Car-free towns are popping up all over Europe, the US, and other countries. This pushes cities and suburbs to be denser, so you don't have to drive miles for every little thing.

One big reason for this change is that we need to deal with global warming. Cars pump out a ton of greenhouse gases – about 12% worldwide, and up to 50% in places like the US where everyone drives everywhere.

Paris had such bad smog that they banned cars with even license plates from driving, or else you'd get fined. It actually cut down on traffic! Turns out, the smog was mostly from everyone using diesel fuel because it was cheaper than gas. After the traffic got better, Paris, which had worse smog than many other capitals, banned cars with odd license plates, too.

Cars have really shaped how everyone lives. But in the US, studies show that fewer people are buying cars, driving, or even getting driver's licenses. Even though America loved cars, people now want a greener world and see the good in not owning a car. Dealing with insurance, payments, repairs, safety, and just the stress of it all is a pain. Sure, cars are useful, but the downsides are becoming clearer. Fewer teens and young adults are getting licenses, and more people are skipping car ownership altogether. So, why even bother owning a car?

People around the world get the good and bad about cars, see the perks of driving less, and are making changes. One of the most important inventions ever is starting to take a backseat.

Across the globe, a shift is happening. People want to cut way down on greenhouse gases. They're trying to live without cars, or at least use them way less. It might sound extreme, but lots of places are already proving it can work and that car pollution can measurably decrease.

Take Vauban, Germany. It's a cool suburb near Freiburg, and it really shows how this can be done. You won't find parking spots, driveways, or garages there. Instead, folks mostly hop on a tram that takes them right into Freiburg. Heidrun Walter, who lives there with her two kids and works as a media trainer, is super happy with how things are. Vauban was finished in 2006, and it's part of a growing trend in Europe and the US: living in the suburbs without needing a car all the time. The place is about a square mile. Its 5,500 residents can easily walk to all the important stores and services. They're all close together on one main street.

Europe makes up about 12 percent of the world's greenhouse gases. But the US? A whopping 50 percent! This hit home in Paris when bad smog led to crazy high pollution. The city had to ban some cars from driving. One day, cars with even-numbered license plates couldn't drive. The next day, it was odd-numbered plates. People who broke the rules had to pay fines. Some drivers were annoyed, but the city made public transportation free for the whole week to make things easier.

Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day without Cars" to get people to use their cars less. Millions of Colombians found other ways to get to work. The city has seven million people. It pushed everyone to try different ways of getting around to fight smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus said that even the rain couldn't stop people from participating. That year, two more Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, joined in the "Day without Cars." According to Enrique Rera, the mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, "These people are making a big change, and it's spreading!" Besides the "Day without Cars," Bogota has also started rush-hour restrictions, improved sidewalks, and added new places to eat and shop.

Whether it's Vauban designing a place where you don't need a car, Paris banning cars for a bit, or Bogota having a day where everyone tries something else, these cities all show us how to use cars less. All over the world, people seem ready to make changes to cut down on greenhouse gases. In the US, fewer people are registering cars. Also, fewer young people have driver's licenses. That number dropped by 23 percent between 2001 and 2009. In the end, the goal is to save resources, lower greenhouse gas emissions, make things safer. With all these cities and communities moving in this direction, it looks like we can actually achieve that goal.

There are several good things that happen when we don't drive as much. For one thing, it cuts down on gases that hurt the environment. You see, cars make up a big chunk of these gases; some studies say it's between 12% and 50% depending on where you live. So, if we don't use cars so much, we get less bad gas and cleaner air. That's way better for the environment!

Also, less driving means less stress, wouldn't you know it? Getting to work every day can take a lot of planning, and thinking about traffic can be really worrying. Believe it or not, some places that made rush hour changes saw traffic get a lot better. This means you can chill out a little if you don't drive as much.

Another cool thing is that younger people aren't driving as much these days. The numbers show that less of them are getting licenses. One study even says that the amount of young drivers went down by almost a quarter. With fewer new drivers, there may be fewer accidents, too. Essentially, it could keep young drivers safe!

To sum it all up, driving less helps everyone. It makes less bad gas, cuts down on stress, and means fewer young people are driving. That's why using your car less really makes our neighborhoods better places to be.

Globally, a trend is emerging. Politicians and regular people alike are pushing to drive less. For instance, there's a German suburb that's mostly car-free. People there seem happy! Germany isn't the only place wanting less pollution and car stress. Paris, France, also wants this "anti-car revolution." When Paris gets super smoggy, and the air is terrible, they stop cars temporarily. After just a week, the air is way better. Vauban, Germany, and Paris, France, are doing different things to cut down on cars. But three cities in Colombia are also in on this! They have a car-free day every year.

Let's look closer at Vauban, Germany. Why did they make a city with almost no cars or parking? Usually, suburbs have houses far apart, no stores or fun stuff nearby, and tons of cars on the roads. This is true for many suburbs. But it also means smog and pollution from all those cars. Vauban fixed this by having fewer cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." She said cars make up about twelve percent of Europe's greenhouse gases. In some parts of the U.S., it's around fifty percent! Vauban gives people cleaner air by reducing car use. Instead of just roads, they made walkways and shops. This makes it easier for people to get around. Plus, they use the money they saved on roads for other ways to get around. The same article said that usually, eighty percent of transportation money went to highways. Only twenty percent went to other options.

Paris, France, is also trying hard to reduce car use. The city has lots to see, and you can bike to most places. So, you don't need to pollute more by driving! That's why Paris sometimes bans cars. The city's smog can be as bad as Beijing, China, which is known for bad pollution. Robert Duffer wrote an article, "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." At first, Paris wanted to ban cars completely to stop the smog. But they knew that wouldn't work with so many drivers. So, they made a deal. On some days, cars with even license plate numbers couldn't drive. The next day, cars with odd numbers were banned. This went on for a week. If you broke the rules, you paid a twenty-two euro fine. Duffer's article said almost four thousand drivers got fined that week. Even with the fines, the ban cut traffic by sixty percent!

Bogota, Colombia, is another city trying to limit cars. Bogota has almost seven million people. They have a car-free day every year. People walk, bike, skate, or take buses. Andrew Selsky wrote an article, "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." He said that by pushing buses, taxis, and other options, the city wants less smog, traffic, and accidents. After three successful years, two other Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, joined in. This car-free day was part of a plan to make Bogota better, starting in the mid-1990s, according to Selsky's article. Because of this plan, Bogota built one hundred and eighteen miles of bike paths. They also added lots of parks and sports centers.

Even in the U.S., where people drive a lot, things might be changing. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article, "The End Of Car Culture." She said that over the last eight years, fewer young people are getting licenses and driving. More people are taking buses, taxis, walking, or biking. This is because traffic is getting worse, so these options are often faster than driving. If people do drive, they're carpooling more. This helps reduce air pollution.

A large chunk of folks now want to get rid of the Electoral College. Back in 2000, a Gallup poll showed that people preferred a straight-up popular vote. As Americans, we've got to think about all our choices. The Electoral College used to be great, but now it's clear we need to shake things up.

The Electoral College has a really twisty way of picking electors, which takes months to plan. First, states have to pick their electors, then they've got to plan and hold meetings. Figuring out who should be an elector and why is a real head-scratcher on voting day. After that, they vote for the President and Vice President. Then comes the vote count. The Electoral College has 538 members, and you need at least 270 votes to win the presidency. A direct election could make all this way simpler.

A direct election would make sure everyone's voice is heard, which could calm things down between the states. People want to feel like they matter. The Electoral College is getting less popular in the US because people think a direct vote is a better way to pick the President and Vice President.

Each person running for office picks a bunch of electors in each state, usually chosen by their political party. So, if a voter doesn't like the electors picked, they might feel like their vote doesn't count and their voice isn't really being heard in the presidential election.

Basically, people don't like the Electoral College anymore. It's a pain in the neck. A lot of people think some government folks don't want to get rid of it because they're afraid of losing power. A direct election would clear up a lot of the fuss and make it way easier to pick our leader.

Since forever, folks have tried to make life easier, from simple hunting tools to horse carriages. Back then, horse poop was the main problem and people were hired to deal with it. But as time went on, we got cars, which are great, but they also brought smog with them.

Recently, Paris said "no" to some cars because the smog was so bad. This has even changed the weather in Paris, making nights cooler and days warmer. Some say it's diesel fuel, but any fuel that burns would make smog. On the other hand, there's Vauban, Germany. It's a place where people don't use cars. They don't even own them because the public transportation is so good! People have moved to Vauban and say they're way less stressed than when they had cars. Kids can play outside without worrying about cars, so they might not need to use their phones as much. Vauban might seem extreme, but Bogota, Colombia has a car-free day once a year. Only buses and taxis are allowed. If you break the rules, you get a fine. Even in the U.S., young people are driving less. From 2003 to 2009, there was a 23% drop. There are so many other things to do that driving isn't as important anymore. Maybe this is the start of a change. In April 2013, the average miles driven was like it was in 1995, when fewer people had cars. New York City even has a carpool program!

But in some places, like Florida, the public transportation isn't so great. If you wanted to get from Homestead to Miami, you might have to drive to a bus stop, take a bus to a train station, ride the train for an hour, and then take another bus for half an hour. That's about three hours each way, or thirty hours a week! Driving would only take an hour, so you kind of need a car there. Just running errands in downtown Miami or Doral from Homestead takes all day by car. Without a car, it could take days or you'd have to wake up super early.

If you look at Vauban, you see people are less stressed than those who rely on cars. They talk to their neighbors more because they walk around, so they feel more like a community. Plus, the air is cleaner. They don't have to breathe in smog every day like people in cities do. Cars cause about 12% of Europe's pollution and over 50% in the United States. Paris could have fixed its smog problem sooner if they had paid attention. After World War II, we spent a lot of time and money on cars. Imagine what we could have done if we had focused on medicine or other things. In the U.S., most of the money for transportation goes to highways, not public transportation. Places like Vauban and Bogota work because shops and houses are close together, so buses can get around easily. It's hard to stop using cars because we're so used to them, but we can change little by little.

Friends, countrymen, lend me your ears! I'm worried, truly worried, about how dirty our air is getting here in America. But, hey, I think I have an idea that might actually work: let's drive our cars less! Seriously, this simple move could slash pollution big time – it's like hitting the pollution problem right where it hurts, since cars are the second biggest cause. Plus, and this is cool, it could bring us closer together and make our towns way more fun places to live.

Like I said, cars are a major pollution source. So, cutting back on driving means less gunk going into the air. Less car exhaust equals less carbon messing with our atmosphere. And guess what? That could mean we all breathe a little easier and live a little healthier. We've already seen how great it can be in places that don't have a lot of cars. And get this, events like "Car-Free Day" are catching on in places like Columbia and other Central American countries. Less car exhaust also means less smog – that nasty stuff that makes the air thick and gross. By reducing car fumes, we can avoid serious pollution problems that cities like Paris have.

But wait, there's more! Driving less can actually make our social lives better. Imagine walking around, chatting with your neighbors, or biking to the park. People in car-free zones, like Vauban, say they're way less stressed without the whole car thing. And get this: younger folks aren't even that into cars! They'd rather take the bus or carpool, which means more time hanging out with friends and family.

Think about it: because we're always driving, sidewalks and bike paths get ignored. But if we ditch the car more often, those broken sidewalks and bumpy roads will get fixed, pronto. In Columbia, "Car-Free Day" led to fixing sidewalks and building a ton of new bike lanes – over a hundred miles! Plus, they built more parks, sports fields, and cool shopping spots. So, less driving can help us fix up our towns, make more parks, and create more fun places to hang out. And, it can keep us safer by reducing the chance of getting hit by a car and making our streets safer overall.

So, my fellow Americans, please think about joining me in driving less. The benefits are huge – cleaner air, healthier lives, and stronger communities for all of us!

Decreasing car dependence offers huge perks, even if it means tweaking commutes. Less driving? Think cleaner air in smoggy spots like L.A., Beijing, and Paris, less stress for drivers, and major savings. Who doesn't like saving cash?

Smog is a growing headache in packed, polluted zones. The hazy air over L.A. is hard to miss, and Beijing often sits under a toxic cloud. Cars are big culprits, spewing greenhouse gases straight up. In Europe, cars cause 15% of these emissions. But in the U.S.? A whopping 50%! Cut car use, cut pollution. Can't ditch cars completely? A hybrid like a Prius helps. Some places, like Bogota, go all-out with "Car-Free Days," a total hit that's spreading. These days seriously cut smog. Paris even had to ban cars due to crazy smog. Hybrids and carpools were okay, showing how extreme things get in smoggy cities.

Driving is a real stressor. Rush hour jams mess up schedules and fray nerves. Drivers sweat getting cut off and fear accidents. Imagine ditching that stress completely! Vauban, Germany, goes car-free to chill things out. Residents own cars, but park them in a pricey garage on the edge of town (think $40,000!). Everything's walkable – shops, food, you name it. Cars are mostly for long trips and highways. Walking to get mail or enjoy nature without car worries cuts stress big time. No need to constantly watch out for the cars going to a favorite restaurant! Plus, with phones and the internet, close-by is less important for keeping in touch. Platforms like Facebook, Twitter, Snapchat, and Instagram make it easy to connect, which takes some strain off the need to drive.

Finally, fewer cars mean more money. In car-free spots like Vauban, you might not need a car at all. A decent car can cost $30-40k. Think of what you could do with that kind of money! A nice home, a dream trip, maybe even college for your kid. And everything’s still a quick walk away. Not a bad trade, right?

Both the electoral college and the popular vote offer ways to pick a winner in an election. But, a popular vote might have more problems. The electoral college is an easier way to pick a president, so there's less confusion. Sure, it has some downsides, but the good things about the electoral college are usually better than the bad.

A popular vote does have some good points. Richard A. Posner says in "In Defense of the Electoral College" that the electoral college uses electors to pick the president, not just the regular people ("source 3 a15"). But, a popular vote would let the people pick the president themselves, not through others. Also, voters often don't know who the electors are that they're voting for. Bradford Plumer says in "The Indefensible Electoral College" that states pick the electors, and voters don't get to choose ("source 2 a 10"). So, people don't have much say in who gets elected.

Even though people don't have much control over who gets picked, electors usually vote the way the people in their state voted. The electoral college also makes it easier to know who won the election. It's less likely to have a tie with this system. Posner says that "The winning candidate's share of the electoral college invariably exceeds his share of the popular vote," and even if someone wins the popular vote by a little bit, they still get all the electoral votes from that state ("source 3 a 15"). This makes it clear who won.

Besides being organized, the electoral college makes candidates talk to different groups of people in different areas. Posner says, "Not one region has enough votes to win an election, so the president has to be liked by other regions as well" ("source 3 a 19"). This means a candidate needs to get support from many people across the country to win. Swing states are also important because they have voters who pay attention and think about who they're voting for. Posner thinks that "People in swing states decide the election and are the most thoughtful voters" ("source 3 a 20"). So, candidates have to listen to what these voters care about.

Some people might like a popular vote better, but the electoral college is more organized and a better way to elect presidents. It helps keep things in order and makes candidates get to know the people by talking to different groups and areas.

Dear Rick Scott, the system called the Electoral College used in recent elections, has caused controversy. This body has 538 votes. A candidate needs at least 270 votes to win. Big states have more votes. California has 55 votes, while Rhode Island has only 3. Since 2000 when Al Gore lost despite winning the popular vote, the Electoral College has faced debate. Some want to get rid of it, but I think it should stay for presidential elections. It gives a clear winner, guarantees a president for "Everyone", gives voters in swing states influence, and prevents runoff elections.

Without it, picking a winner could be hard. The system always picks a victor. Even if there is a tie in votes, the Senate decides. Each senator votes, so smaller states have less power. It also makes candidates appeal to different regions. This means the president represents the whole nation, not just one area. Also, swing states matter a lot. States like Florida, New York, Texas, and California have big influence. Winning these states helps win the presidency. These states matter because of their big populations, but also because of their informed voters. Swing states have more educated voters who know what is going on and understand the candidates. The Electoral College also stops a situation where no candidate gets a majority. Bill Clinton won with only 43% of the vote, but got 370 electoral votes. It mainly removes the need for a runoff. Despite claims that the Electoral College should be removed because voters vote for electors, it makes sure there is a clear winner, which is key.

So, the Electoral College should stay in the system. With 538 votes, needing 270 to win, and its tie-breaking rule, it always picks a winner. It ensures a victor, elects "Everyone's President," acknowledges educated voters in swing states, and keeps us away from runoff elections.

Cars play a huge role in everyone's lives all over the world, as many can agree. Whether you think they're important or not, cutting back on how much we depend on cars would be great for us and the planet. If cities can handle car use well, they'll see lots of good stuff happen, like less smog, safer roads with fewer crashes, and a quieter, calmer vibe.

"Passenger cars are responsible for 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe...and up to 50 percent in some car-intensive areas in the United States. paragraph 5" Cars are a big cause of pollution, especially in busy cities where there's tons of traffic. One writer points out that "..some new suburbs may well look more Vaubanlike not only in developed countries, but also in the developing world, where emissions from an increasing number of private cars owned by the burgeoning middle class are choking cities" Experts mostly agree that using cars less is a good way to fight air pollution. For example, in Paris, "After days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city" (paragraph 10, source two). So, if we have fewer cars on the road in cities, we'll have less smog and cleaner air, which means better health for everyone and the environment.

Sadly, car accidents happen every day, and people die. Some crashes can't be stopped, but others could be. Just think about how many lives are lost because of accidents that happen in crowded cities or because drivers are stressed and drive like crazy. One person said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way" (paragraph 3). For a lot of people, driving makes them feel stressed and uptight. So, if we didn't rely on cars so much, we could all feel a lot calmer. Imagine a nicer place where you hear kids playing and bikes going by instead of loud car horns, engines, and crashes.

Roads take up way too much space! If we walked and biked more, and drove less, we wouldn't need so many roads, and we could have bigger sidewalks. Cities are trying harder to find ways to get people to use cars less, "...in attempts to make suburbs more compact..." One idea is to put stores closer together on main streets so people can walk to them instead of driving to malls far away (paragraph 6). This would make things safer for everyone.

Basically, using cars less is a win-win. It means less pollution, fewer accidents, and nicer, quieter places for us all to live. If more cities and countries worked on cutting back on car use, it would really make the world a better place and improve our everyday lives.

Do we depend on cars too much?

Like in Source 2, "Paris bans driving due to smog," the city stopped some cars because of bad air. It was super polluted for days, almost a record! We use cars too much, which makes pollution worse and hurts nature. But, we can change! Less driving can be good in many ways.

Can you live well without a car? In VAUBAN, Germany, there's a town with no cars! They're trying to see if no cars makes people happier and healthier. Driving every day can be stressful with traffic, loud noise, and crashes. Sources 1 and 3 say, "Because of this, 70% of families there don't have cars, and 57% sold their cars to move there. "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way," said Heidrun Walter." This shows that not having a car can make people feel better.

Another good thing about less driving is less pollution. After the air was super bad, Paris banned some cars to clean up the smog. Almost 4,000 drivers got tickets, and some even lost their cars for a bit because they were mad about the tickets. But, it worked! Traffic in Paris went down by 60% in just a few days. The air got better, so the French government let some cars drive again on certain days.

Are Americans liking cars less? Or, is technology making driving less needed? Elisabeth Rosenthal thinks that "the internet lets people work from home and talk to friends without driving. Cities are getting nicer, so people don't want to live in the suburbs anymore. Also, phones and ride-sharing apps make it easier to get around, like with shared vans to work" (source 4, 35). Because of new technology, people don't have to meet in person. They can use apps, calls, texts, and video to talk instead.

Because of all these things, I think less driving can be good for us. I hope you think so too!

Beeping and honking, they come in a rainbow—red, blue, green, even basic black and white. Cars: they're everywhere, hauling folks from A to B. Imagine if they vanished. What good could come of a car-free life? Turns out, ditching cars could be a win for both our planet and our minds.

For starters, less cars equals a healthier Earth. Get this: "Cars spew out 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases...and a whopping 50% in some car-crazy spots in the U.S.!" In other words, cars are big-time polluters, heating up the globe. Paris tried a temporary driving ban to fight smog. Guess what? "Traffic jams dropped by 60% in Paris after five days of nasty smog!" Bottom line: less cars, less pollution, a safer planet. And it seems like things are headed that way. "Young people drove 23% less between 2001 and 2009." Experts think this trend will stick and could "help cut carbon emissions, since transportation is a huge source of America's pollution."

But there's more! Cutting back on cars can boost our mental and physical health. Check out Vauban, a German town where folks live car-free. One resident said, "I was always stressed with a car. I'm much happier now." Less time stuck in traffic, less road rage—that's a recipe for less stress. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars," and people love it. One person said, "It's a good way to de-stress." Stress reduction is a big plus for mental well-being. And it doesn't stop there. In Bogota, the car-free day gets people to "walk, bike, skate, or bus to work." Exercise makes you happy, releasing feel-good chemicals in your brain. Less cars could mean more exercise, which means happier, healthier people.

So, fewer cars could mean a healthier planet and happier people. Sure, cars are handy, but they come with a cost. Next time you reach for your keys, think about the perks of leaving the car behind. Remember the feeling of fresh air—cars take that away. And remember that cars can make you angry and stressed.

Why does traffic always seem jammed, especially around 5 PM? Mostly, it's because we rely too much on cars. Driving less has lots of good effects and could really make things better.

At first, cars seem fast for getting around. But this one good thing doesn't make up for all the bad stuff. One big problem is that driving all the time makes us lazy. Walking or running is good exercise, but we miss out when we drive everywhere. A guy named Walter said he was always stressed when he had a car but felt much happier without one. We should try to drive less and find fun, relaxing ways to get around. Walking might take longer, but it's worth it in the long run. No traffic jams, and you can go places cars can't. Plus, you can take different routes, not just roads. Rain is the main reason people don't walk. But the mayor of Bogota, Antanas Mockus, said rain doesn't stop people from doing stuff outside. Cars keep you dry, but they're also more dangerous than walking. Cities like Germany, Paris, and Bogota are trying out "car-free" ways of life because of this.

Also, if we drove less, there might be less road rage. You often see drivers yelling and fighting. Cars cause this. Driving less might make people less stressed on the road. Car accidents are a big problem, and lots of people die because of them. Driving less could save lives. Cars are mostly bad because they're not safe. Walking and biking are smoother, easier ways to travel with fewer accidents. This would also help with traffic, making it easier to get places. Driving drunk is another big problem that causes many deaths. If a drunk person was walking instead of driving, they probably wouldn't hurt anyone. Think about the bad things about driving so much.

Lastly, Barack Obama wanted us to drive less to cut down on pollution from cars in the U.S. Reports say Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less, and not getting licenses as much, which means we're making progress. Paris banned cars because of bad smog. The air was so polluted that they had to stop some people from driving. Diesel fuel was a big reason for the smog. In France, 67% of vehicles use diesel, but it's only 53% in other Western European countries. Bogota wants to "help people use other ways to get around and reduce smog." If you break the rules, you get a fine. They saw that driving less could make people less stressed and improve the air. Bill Ford wants cities where "walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses are all connected to save time, use less fuel, make the air cleaner, and keep people safe." These are just some of the good things about driving less. Changing to a life with fewer cars would be great. We should stop pollution, prevent accidents, and stop underage driving. Think about the bad things about cars and how they compare to the good things about driving less.

For quite some time now, we have been battling global warming and pollution. People often ask, why can’t we just end this fight quickly? Well, the big reason is that we still rely so heavily on cars. It is important for everyone to find ways to use their cars less. Some places have already started putting limits in place. For instance, Vauban, Germany has banned cars altogether; Paris, France only allows certain cars on the road based on license plate numbers on different days; and there's Bogota, Colombia.

One way to cut down on car use could be like what Vauban, Germany did: build cities without driveways or parking spaces. To do this, people would need to buy expensive parking garage spots. A source mentions that cars create about twelve percent of the greenhouse gases in Europe. It might not seem like much, but when you look at all the pollution, that percentage makes a big difference. But if we created cities where cars weren't really needed, we’d see less pollution because people wouldn't drive as much. Heidrun Walter notes, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way," and this points to how much less stressed people can be without a car. By not having to spend on gas and car repairs, people have less money worries, leading to an easier life. So, having cities where people have to pay for parking garage spaces is a good way to reduce pollution and make residents happier.

Another idea is to do what Paris, France did, which was to ban cars with odd-numbered license plates on Mondays, and even-numbered plates on Tuesdays. They did this because pollution levels were super high, even worse than in Beijing, China. But, one source mentions, Paris saw pollution drop by sixty percent in just five days after starting this odd-even system. The fast and drastic drop proves that limiting cars through these kinds of restrictions actually works.

A third option is "no car days," which Bogota, Colombia practices. Carlos Arturo Plaza explains, "It's a good way to take away stress and lower air pollution," showing the connection between cars and pollution, and highlighting the good things about car-free days. The source also points out that other Colombian cities, like Cali and Valledpar, are also doing this. Also, leaders from other countries visit Colombia to see what it's like, and they often seem really impressed. This means the idea could spread to other countries and have a global impact. Clearly, this is another effective approach to reducing car use.

The truth is cars are a big problem, yet many people still use them without really thinking about how much they are adding to pollution. Some good ways to reduce car use are building cities with paid parking, doing odd-even license plate rules, and having "no car days."

The United States belongs to its people, governed by its people, and exists for its people. We pick our leaders through voting, which is the fairest way to choose the president and vice president.

The electoral college? Not so fair. Electors might know a lot about the country and think they know what's best. However, they are far removed from the daily lives of normal people. Regular folks better understand the problems they face. Like Bradford Plummer said, "the best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality" (source 14). These electors, deep in politics, may know the ins and outs of Washington but often miss the struggles of everyday people.

People want a say in their government. If only the electoral college chose the president, folks would feel left out and like they have no control. Keeping the peace requires that people agree. Without it, things would fall apart, and our country's base would be gone. Richard A. Posner points out that citizens would "feel that their votes do not count, that the new president will have no regard for their interest, that he really isn't their president" (source 19). If they are upset when their person loses now, just think how mad they'd be if they didn't get to vote at all.

The electoral college just isn't fair. Richard A. Posner even said that some argue to keep it because of "its lack of democratic pedigree" (source 17). Since the popular vote works well, why change it? Sure, change can be good, but messing with this basic part of our democracy? Not needed.

Basically, ditching the popular vote would be wrong, unfair, and just plain bad. It goes against everything "we the people" stands for.

Even with some pretty big problems, lots of things keep the Electoral College going. Each state gets a number of electors equal to their folks in the House plus their two Senators. Add 'em all up, and you get 538 electors. To win the White House, you need more than half – at least 270 votes. People often say the Electoral College is good because it makes sure we know who wins, helps the president represent everyone, matters in swing states, gives big states a voice, and stops us from needing run-off elections.

Basically, the Electoral College is there to make sure we pick the president the right way, keeping outsiders from messing things up. It's how a candidate actually wins. But, it's not perfect. One big worry is that it seems unfair because of the "winner-takes-all" thing. Candidates might spend more time in bigger states because they have more people, which means more electoral votes. Another issue? You can win the popular vote but still lose the election. It all comes down to convincing the Electoral College, and that's not always a sure thing, even if most people voted for you.

One reason people like the Electoral College is that it's supposed to make the winner clear. It's said to cut down on arguments about the popular vote because the electors vote the way they’re supposed to. Also, it makes sure the President has to listen to all parts of the country. No one area has enough votes to elect a president on its own. Another good thing, supposedly, is how it affects swing states, since whoever wins there gets all the electoral votes. And, it's said to help big states by giving them more power. Because they have more people, they get more elected officials. So, candidates tend to pay more attention to what big states want. Lastly, it keeps us from having run-off elections by making it less likely no one gets enough votes.

A lot of things keep the Electoral College going, but it definitely has its weaknesses. Still, some folks think it's the best way to pick a president. They argue that just counting the popular vote would be like letting people who don't know much about the country decide. There are lots of rules for elections, but supporters think this way works best for the nation. It's worth thinking about these reasons why people want to keep the Electoral College around.

Dear State Senator, I'm writing to urge a change in how we pick the President. Let's switch to a system where the person with the most votes wins, ditching the Electoral College. My idea is straightforward: the regular people, not a small bunch of electors, should be the ones who decide who leads our country.

Plus, lots of important people have said the same thing about getting rid of the Electoral College, like Nixon, Carter, Dole, and even the U.S. Chamber of Commerce. The worry about things going wrong is a big deal. It doesn't seem right that electors with their own opinions could affect who becomes president.

Think about when Obama and Romney ran. Obama had way more electoral votes, 29 compared to Romney's 3. That big difference shows how unfair things could be, stopping the real choice of the people from happening. A popular vote would make sure things are more fair for everyone.

Sure, the Electoral College helps when no one gets most of the votes, like when Nixon won in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 with only 43 percent. It stopped things from getting too messy. But, it still gave people other than the popular vote a big say in who should be president. I think that choice belongs to the American people.

Thank you, State Senator, for thinking about what I said about picking the President: should we keep the Electoral College or switch to the popular vote?

Dear State Senator, some say the Electoral College works well, saying it forces candidates to campaign in swing states and get votes from smaller states, as Source 3 says in "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing a president." But I don't agree. I think it's wrong and unfair to the people.

First off, Source 2, "The Indefensible Electoral College: Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong," shows how unfair it is to voters. Because most states use a winner-take-all system, candidates only care about close races in "swing" states, ignoring others. This means a candidate can be at a disadvantage, yet still win a state just because of how the Electoral College works. Voters might not even know what the winner stands for until they're already president! This lack of connection could mean the winning candidate doesn't really support that state. Also, Source 3 claims, "The Electoral College requires a presidential candidate to have transregional appeal. No region has enough electoral votes to elect a president. So a solid regional favorite, such as Rodney in the South, has no incentive to campaign heavily in those states, for he gains no electoral votes by increasing his plurality in states that he knows he will win." If Mitt Rodney ran against Barack Obama, Obama's strength in the North would hurt Rodney, which is what the Electoral College wants.

So, I think the Electoral College is a bad and unjust way to pick presidents. Source 2 backs this up, saying, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational. The best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality," which means the Electoral College doesn't fit today's democracy and people doubt if it even works.

Folks often slam the Electoral College, saying it's not a fair way to vote and should be canned. In this setup, you vote for "electors," who then pick the candidate with the most votes. Some think this stinks because it stops people from directly picking their leader. Instead, we use a system where someone can win without getting most of the votes, with electors supposedly doing what the people want. A lot of people think the Electoral College is weird and should be dumped.

It's also said that many voters don't know much about the electors they're picking. Some folks in certain states say they don't see enough ads or news about the election, making it hard to make good choices. Presidential candidates usually focus on states they think they can win over, trying to get noticed and win support. While a president should appeal to more than just a certain area, this can make people in less important states feel like their votes don't matter. Even though people think their vote goes straight to a candidate, it actually goes to the electors. So, winning the popular vote doesn't mean you'll win the presidency, making a more direct say preferable.

"Electors" are chosen by parties to vote for their person. Though rare, the Electoral College winner can lose the popular vote, with Gore receiving more votes but losing to Bush in 2000, a situation unseen since 1888. Yet, it could happen again, introducing the possibility of winning or losing despite the vote count. This can cause fights, as each state is winner-takes-all. However, it helps avoid situations where nobody gets a majority, which could mean more elections. By always picking a clear winner, the Electoral College can fix this. People want their vote to mean something, not just to express a political opinion, but this is often wrong.

The Electoral College is often seen as a bad way to pick leaders, feeling unnatural and not right. It's meant to help people get involved, but it can seem like it messes with the process, making individual votes matter less. In a vote-based democracy, making smart choices is key. The system should make sure that people can vote knowing their vote really counts.

Dear State Senator, I'm writing to say I really think we should keep the Electoral College. Changing to just using the popular vote for picking presidents? I don't think we need to do that. The Electoral College is great because it lets the House of Representatives help pick the president, which makes things fairer and stops election messes. It also helps us understand voting and who our new president is.

What's really cool about the Electoral College is that the House of Representatives gets to help pick our president. Source One, paragraph three, says that how many electors a state gets is the same as how many people it has in Congress: one for each House member, plus two for the Senators. This means we're not alone in picking the president. Also, paragraph five of that same source says that "The electors are usually picked by the candidate's political party," which shows the House isn't the only group that helps with elections. The government plays a big role in the Electoral College, since its votes matter a lot.

The Electoral College also makes sure voting is fair. Source Three says that "The Electoral College fixes the problem where big states get too much power because the Senate isn't fair..." This means we don't have to worry too much about how the president is voted for and picked. Plus, that same source and paragraph says, "So, if everything else is the same, big states get more attention from presidential candidates than small states do..." This means not everyone needs to stress about who the president will be. The Electoral College is super helpful, especially for bigger states.

Most importantly, the Electoral College stops election problems. Source Three, paragraph twenty-two, says that "The Electoral College stops problems in elections where no candidate gets most of the votes." This means the Electoral College is good at keeping votes easy to understand. Also in that same source and paragraph, "There's no need for extra elections when no one wins most of the votes; the Electoral College makes sure there's always a clear winner..." That tells me the Electoral College helps keep votes accurate.

Some folks don't like the Electoral College because their favorite candidate might not win. But that's not a good reason to hate it, because it keeps things organized when we're picking our president. Source Two, paragraph Ten, says that "With the Electoral College, we don't vote for the president, but for electors, who then pick the president." This can cause arguments because some think the Electoral College doesn't vote directly for their choice. But they should know that the electors vote based on how many states support their candidate. Also, Source Two says, "Basically, the Electoral College is unfair. Because of the winner-takes-all system, candidates only focus on close races in 'swing' states." While this worries some, the Electoral College doesn't just count votes in big or small states. Its main job is to help the government pick the best president.

In short, I really think we should keep the Electoral College because it helps both us and the government. It makes voting fairer, stopping mistakes and confusion. And most importantly, it cuts down on election problems, so everyone's happy with the results. The Electoral College is a great way to keep elections clear and organized every year. I don't think we need to change anything because, even though the government helps a lot, our votes still matter. Our votes are just as important to the Electoral College as the government's votes.

The Electoral College we have now? Yeah, it's pretty old-school. Back in the day, when tech was basic, it was a way to count votes from all the states fairly. Each state sent electors to vote, which made sense then. But now? Tech's way better, making the Electoral College clunky and easy to mess with. Imagine being a Republican in a super-Democrat state; your vote feels like it doesn't even matter, right? Instead of the Electoral College, people might want a national vote where their voice, however small, could actually be heard. Plus, how most states do things – winner takes all – gives big states all the attention, leaving smaller states in the dust with less info, making voters guess instead of knowing what's up.

Think about it: the telegraph was the high-tech stuff when the Electoral College started. Instead of states counting votes and sending electors (who were supposed to be unbothered) to lug those results all over the place, they needed a better plan. Sending people on horses with paper votes? Disaster waiting to happen! Papers gone, riders hurt, entire areas uncounted. The Electoral College? It was a smart fix and a necessity. But just like the telegraph is history, so is the Electoral College. Today, a cell phone? Holds millions of votes. Computers crunch numbers in a blink, giving you stats, answers, and even business ideas instantly. We zap votes wirelessly, so even tiny errors get caught, saved, and fixed. A way better, safer, and more exact method? A computer program to count everything! Each place could send info to a main computer, where it's checked a million times before the final result. To really stop cheating, spread the work across many computers. So, the Electoral College is just a pointless thing that some politicians won't let go of. It's an old relic.

And let's be real, the Electoral College is just plain unfair to lots of folks. Picture this: a Democrat lives in a state that's all Republican. They vote, but the Republican votes win. Bam! That Democrat's vote, and their family's, and every other Democrat in that state? Trash. Gone. Every. Single. Election. With a popular vote, those votes would count towards their candidate. At least then, the votes would be worth something. The winner-takes-all thing makes it even worse. If a candidate gets just over half the votes in California, they get ALL of California's electoral votes, ignoring almost half the people who voted for someone else! That’s hardly what most of the state wants. Big states like California, Texas, and Florida get a boost because they have more electoral votes, while small states like Alaska or Hawaii? Not so much. So, politicians focus on the big guys, and the little guys get ignored. Voters in places like the Dakotas or Montana might not know enough to make a good choice, so they just pick a party. The biggest fights, with tons of ads and noise, happen in the biggest states. Sure, ads have their biases, but at least those voters get more of an idea about the candidates. Swing states, where it's anyone's game, also get flooded with info as candidates fight for their votes. Meanwhile, small states, or states that always vote the same way, get skipped, leaving voters to guess and their votes might not even matter. A popular vote would make candidates go everywhere, stop the big-state favoritism, and make voting fairer for everyone.

Basically, the Electoral College might have been the best way to vote back in the 1700s and 1800s, but now? It's old and not fair. We have better ways to talk and travel, so the Electoral College is not just out of date, but also biased, not practical, and bad for democracy. Time to use new tech and ditch the Electoral College for good.

Worldwide, folks are using cars less and less. Ditching cars? Great idea! Less pollution, more money in your pocket. People are changing their ways because they want to or because they're trying to clean up their neighborhoods. Places like China, Spain, and even the good old U.S.A. are putting the brakes on car use. Not everyone's on board, but many are pushing for it because, well, pollution stinks! If half the world got serious about this, think of the air we'd save. Cars are a big part of life, sure, but they're also a big part of the problem.

In Vauban, Germany, it's like cars never existed. You *can* own one, but good luck finding a spot, and it'll cost ya! Forty grand just to park at home? Ouch! No wonder so many ditch their rides. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about it: "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." Guess what? Seventy percent of families don't have a car, and over half sold theirs just to move there. Shows you the good and bad of going car-free. Saving money? Big time! No car payments, no crazy parking fees, no gas guzzling. Plus, cleaner air.

Paris, France? Smog city! So, they banned some cars. Robert Duffer explains: if your license plate ended in an even number, stay home! Otherwise, €22 fine. Next day, odd numbers. Boom, pollution went down. And with all those Parisians, that's saying something! The goal? Cleaner air, healthier lungs. Duffer said, "4,000 drivers were fined," and "27 people had their cars impounded." Traffic? Down sixty percent! Once the air cleared, the ban was gone.

Bogota, Columbia, turned a little thing into something big. Andrew Selsky tells the tale: "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Started small, now it's a trend.

One day a year, no cars! Except buses and taxis, of course. Seven million people trying to breathe easier. Alternative transport and less smog, that's the aim. Break the rules? Fines, fines, fines! Selsky says Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" Selsky 27 That's a whole lot of bikes. Parks got better, sports got cleaner, and the air? Fresher. Now other cities are copying them.

Rosenthal writes about the U.S. too. "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. Great news for "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. New York City? Taxis and subways galore! Parking is insane, so many skip the car altogether. Think of the cash saved! Traffic's still a mess, but imagine it with even more cars. The city runs on public transit. It's pricey enough as it is, and without buses and trains, the air would be choked.

So, less cars? Good stuff all around. Smog fighting, cleaner air, saving dough – ditching the car is a win-win. Walk if you can! Good for your wallet, your health, and the planet. Cars are fast, but think about the cost, in money and pollution. Embrace the car-free life! See the benefits, reap the rewards.

Car companies have enjoyed booming business as the car industry grows quickly, and profits have increased. But, we need to really think about the good things that can come from driving less in our towns. Studies show that driving a lot makes people feel way more stressed. As Carlos Arturo Plaza said in Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," using cars less can make us less stressed. It's great if each person tries to drive less, but it makes a bigger difference when everyone in the town does it together. Even though people are still driving a lot in America, fewer people are buying cars, which means many are trying out other ways to get around, like carpooling. Taking the bus might take longer, but it's worth it. If we drive less or not at all in our towns, lots of cool stuff and good things can happen.

Cars are a big reason for pollution around the world, making the air dirtier and hurting the ozone layer. We're used to having cars because they're easy to use; getting a driver's license at 16 feels like becoming free. But, we often don't think about the bad stuff that comes with owning cars, like how we use fossil fuels to drive. Just think how much better it would be if we didn't use cars as much. If we depended less on cars, it would seriously cut down on the damage to the ozone layer, which would let us breathe cleaner air, just like in places where they're already doing this. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," shows how well these bans can work. Paris, which is known for being very polluted, stopped people from driving sometimes to help clean up the air a bit. The city gets super polluted because cold nights and warm days trap the car fumes, and the car ban was supposed to fix that. After they stopped people from driving, the air in the city got better. The article said, "Paris typically has more smog than other European capitals... Last week Paris had 147 ,micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with the 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London..."

No one says we should never use cars, but if you really look at the bad stuff they cause, it might be worse than the good stuff. People in Vauban, Germany, get this and have changed their lives because of it. Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," talks about how people in that town live without cars. The people there say they're happy they got rid of cars because they don't have to deal with traffic jams and accidents. The town government helped make this happen by not having any street parking, driveways, or garages at homes. It might sound weird, but you don't need those things if you don't have cars. Instead, the town spent money on better sidewalks and places that are easy to walk to, with stores close by. One person in Vauban, who trains people in media and has two kids, said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way."
