In the past, the government spent 80 percent of its money on highways and only 20 percent on other ways to get around, but that's going to change in the next six years because they're planning smarter.

So, when we think about getting around in a city, there's a lot to think about. But, if we drive less, we can get a bunch of good stuff, like the government having more money for other things, better air, people feeling better, and saving money. We have to ask ourselves if using cars all the time is worth it when it causes so many problems.

For many Americans, it is very typical to own a car. People see them as great things that let you go places fast, and are simpler than walking. However, when everyone drives cars, roads get jammed with stopped cars. Also, using cars hurts the earth and makes the air dirty. It’s better to not use cars so much because they hurt both the earth and people's health.

Using cars less has good sides like a healthier earth and feeling better. Someone said that "Cars make up 12 percent of the gases that warm the earth in Europe... and even 50 percent in some... places in the United States." Cars directly create these gases, so if we share cars or use buses, we can make less of these gases. Also, walking or biking helps us stay fit.

Cars make air dirty with smog that hangs over cities and is full of bad stuff. This can make people sick and cause more asthma in cities. In a city like Beijing, China, car smog makes it hard to see. "Paris stopped some cars from driving to clean up the air." This shows that Paris did something similar to deal with this. Using fewer cars would make these problems less bad and cut down on smog.

Besides dirty air, driving can make water and land dirty. Cars use gasoline, which is dug up from the earth. Digging for it can hurt the earth and cause oil spills that harm the ocean and animals. If we use trains, buses, walking, or biking, we can use less gasoline from cars. Cars use a lot of gas, so using them less would lower the need for it.

People say life is easier and less stressful without a car, because you don't deal with traffic or crashes. One person said during a "Day Without Cars" in Bogota, Colombia: "'It's great to have less stress and less dirty air,' said businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza." That shows how using cars less can cut down on stress. Someone else said, "'When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way,' said Heidrun Walter." This person in a German town without cars is much happier now. Less stress is another good part of using cars less.

Some people only need a car if there are no good buses or trains. Someone said, "They work around walking, buses, or sharing cars," and "'A car is just to go from A to B when the bus isn't good.'" If we don't use cars when we don't need to, we can have a better system that uses cars less and improves other ways to get around. Better buses can cut down on car use, which is good for the earth and how we live.

Using cars less helps the earth and cuts down on stress. Someone said, "Cars are the second biggest thing that makes pollution in America." If we stop driving when we don't need to and use buses or other ways to get around, we can stop a big part of pollution in America. Less pollution, healthier living, and less stress are all good things from using cars less.

Lots of folks are finally seeing how great it is to rely less on cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," shows this with Vauban, Germany, a town that’s mostly ditched cars. Without cars, the place feels more alive, like Heidrun Walter, a Vauban local, said—you hear kids and bikes, not just engines. Instead of car noise and exhaust, the streets are full of life.

Cutting back on cars is a big win for suburbs. Rosenthal's article mentions how, for the last 20 years, there's been a push to pack cities tighter and make them more walkable. The goal is suburbs that are smaller, with great public transit and stores you can easily walk to. That way, you don’t have to drive to run errands. The US EPA is on board with cutting back on cars.

Less smog and air pollution is a real perk of using cars less. Robert Duffer's "Paris bans driving due to smog" talks about Paris banning some cars to fight terrible air pollution. After five days of bad smog, this cut traffic in Paris by 60 percent, bringing pollution levels close to those of Beijing, China, which is known for bad air. By stopping some car travel, Paris lowered air pollution and traffic.

Andrew Selsky's article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota" shows more good news about cutting cars. It talks about Bogota's car-free day, meant to get people to try other ways of getting around and to cut down on smog. This has led to new parks, sports places, better sidewalks, new restaurants, and fancy shops. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, said that riding a tandem bike with his wife helps with stress and less air pollution.

It's clear that there are tons of upsides to using cars less, like less smog and pollution, and having stores closer to home. This isn’t just good for us, but for the whole planet and future generations.

The wheel's creation opened doors for cars. Nowadays, tons of cars are made each year, making it easier for people to get around in cities and across the world. But, gas-powered cars create carbon dioxide, a gas that hurts the environment, leading to problems like ozone damage and acid rain. Also, too many cars in one place cause traffic jams, among other issues. A lot of debt also changes the number of cars out there. If we want to help the planet, we need to cut back on how many cars we use and make.

The earth is heating up, mainly from the carbon dioxide in the air, and it's already causing problems. Cars add to this pollution a lot. Like it was said in article 1, paragraph 5, "Cars make up 12% of the greenhouse gasses in Europe... and can be up to half in the US". Making and using fewer cars could really help the Earth. This could mean less global warming, helping polar regions last longer and keep animals like penguins and polar bears safe.

Traffic jams stink because you never know when they'll happen and are super annoying. In passage 2, paragraph 14, it points out that "traffic went down 60% in Paris" because they stopped certain license plates on specific days. Less traffic makes it easier to move around. What could be a three-hour wait could become a quick twenty-minute drive. This extra time lets people do other things. Cutting back on cars and traffic can also lower the number of accidents.

Getting a car by leasing or buying can be hard on your wallet. Debt has been a problem since money was invented. Anyone can get into debt. Not everyone can get the newest car models, and it often leads to debt, like article 4, paragraph 32, said, "Americans without cash couldn't get new cars, and the unemployed weren't driving to work anyway". Spending too much can hurt you and the economy. Taking away the debt from car ownership can make the nation’s economy better for many.

To sum up, there are lots of good reasons to use fewer cars. Gas cars mess up the environment, speeding up the melting of ice, harming the ozone, and causing bad weather. Less traffic also makes it easier to get around, letting people move faster and have more time. Plus, less car making and leasing can help the economy a lot. So, cutting back on cars is good for everyone and makes for a better future.

The Electoral College is a pretty old way to pick the President, even though some folks still like it. Basically, it's like a roundabout way of voting. Instead of directly choosing the President, we vote for these "electors," but you can't always be sure they'll stick to who they said they'd vote for. Remember the 2000 election? The Electoral College picked a different winner than the popular vote! Some say it's good because it makes sure we get a clear winner without runoffs, but that doesn't really hold up.

This old system could cause big problems, like what happened in 2000. That election showed us what could go wrong. This disaster potential is a strong reason to ditch it. Back in 1960, some segregationists almost swapped out Democratic electors in Louisiana to go against Kennedy. Plus, electors can change their minds, even though they're supposed to be loyal. Even if it doesn't happen often, it shows a big problem with the system.

It's not just weird; the Electoral College isn't fair. Each state gets at least one vote, so a rep in Wyoming, with only about 500,000 people, has as much power as California's 55 reps, who stand for 35 million voters. Some say ties are rare, but think about 1979: just a few thousand votes shifting in Ohio could've caused one. Because it's "winner-take-all," candidates ignore many states they don't think they can win. In 2000, some states didn't see any candidate visits or ads, so voters didn't even get a chance to support or reject them.

A lot of voters feel left out by the Electoral College, especially after Al Gore won the popular vote but lost the presidency in 2000. People might not bother voting if they think their state is already decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. It's wrong to think voters care more about showing their political colors than believing their vote actually counts. Like we said, individual votes can make a difference. Voting by popular vote might mean more ties, but at least it would free us from this outdated and unreliable system. More than 60 percent of voters don't like the Electoral College, which is a lot. We should pay attention to its problems and try to fix it before something controversial happens again.

It's pretty clear: we've seen failures and close calls, there's a risk of big problems, and it's not fair to voters. Those who defend the Electoral College are off base. The 2000 election and all the unhappy voters show how strange it is to keep using it. This system has been around for a while, and it'll probably stick around for a few more years. But since more people are seeing its flaws, we have a chance to make some good changes.

Cutting back on cars, research says, has great payoffs: less pollution in the air, more people trying other ways to get around, and maybe even a safer place to live.

First off, fewer cars on the road means less air pollution, which is good for everyone's health and the environment. Take Vauban, Germany, for example. Studies there show that cars are a big source of greenhouse gases. In Europe, they cause about 12% of these gases, but in car-crazy parts of the U.S., it can be as high as 50%! Because of this, cities all over the world, like Vauban, Paris, Bogota, and some places in the U.S., are working hard to cut down on car emissions and make things cleaner. Many experts think this trend of driving less will stick around, which is great news for carbon emissions. After all, transportation is the second biggest source of pollution in the U.S. Secondly, when people drive less, they start looking for other, better ways to travel. Instead of just hopping in a car, they might walk to parks, play sports, and get more exercise. Plus, fewer cars mean less traffic. In Bogota, Colombia, they had a car-free day, and everyone walked, biked, skated, or took public transport. The streets were surprisingly clear! In Paris, they even banned some cars to improve air quality, and traffic went down by 60%! During these times, public transport was free from Friday to Monday. All this shows that when we limit car use, good things happen all over the world.

Heidrun Walter, who ditched her car, says she's much happier now and doesn't feel the stress of driving anymore. Similarly, Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, sees cutting back on cars as a way to lower stress and clean up the air. On top of that, less driving makes things safer. M. Sivak's research shows that fewer young people (ages 16 to 39) are getting driver's licenses, while older folks are more likely to keep them. One study found that driving among young people dropped by 23% between 2001 and 2009. This change in how we get around is leading to big improvements in many areas, which is a win-win. The number of miles people drive is almost 9% lower than its peak, and fewer people own cars now than before the economy went bad. Plus, with the way the driving population is changing, it seems like people might not go back to relying on cars as much.

To sum it up, driving less can help the environment, give us more ways to travel, and make our communities safer.

Everyone knows how annoying commuting can be, even if they don't drive! Cars have been the go-to way to get around for a hundred years. But, because we're worrying more about dirty air, fewer people are driving now. If we didn't use cars so much, lots of good things would happen: we'd be less stressed, the air would be cleaner, and life would just be better!

Imagine how much less stressed we'd be without all the traffic and having to be on time all the time. We could bike, take the bus or subway, or even walk! One study talks about Vauban, Germany, where there are hardly any cars. People there say they feel less stressed because everything they need is close enough to walk to.

Cars also hurt the environment by messing up the air. Cars make up a big chunk of the gases that cause global warming – 12% in Europe! And in some places in the US where everyone drives, it's as high as 50%! Paris is a good example of how bad too many cars can be for the air. One study shows that Paris used to have air as smoggy as Beijing, China. So, they had to stop people from driving until the air got better.

Not using cars as much doesn't just help the environment; it helps everyone. Another study talks about Bogota, Colombia, and their car-free day every year. They found out that it really cuts down on smog, and people love it! Even when it rains, lots of people go to bike races and other fun stuff. Because it worked so well, two other cities in Colombia are doing it too. Bogota has the most bike paths in Latin America – 118 miles! It's not just the government pushing for fewer cars; people are doing it themselves. One source says that people are driving 9% less than they used to. Maybe it's because more people are working from home thanks to the internet, so they don't need to drive as much.

Basically, things are changing, maybe for the better. We're not relying on cars as much, and that means less pollution, less stress, and a better life for everyone!

Under the Westminster system, who leads a country is picked by a majority in its parliament. But with a popular vote, the whole country votes. The popular vote allows for different ways of voting, unlike the Westminster way. Since people can be swayed by bad leaders, it's key to stop power from being misused when everyone gets to vote. To keep freedom safe and make sure the people rule, the U.S. needs the Electoral College.

America is the oldest republic around today. This is because the people who started the country were smart. The Electoral College was partly based on the British system and the Iroquois Confederacy, though the Iroquois part is often forgotten because of old biases. Like in England, where the Prime Minister is chosen by parliament, the Founders wanted a way to pick leaders. They worried a leader chosen directly by the people could ruin democracy. So, they made the President responsible to Congress. Congress can impeach the President for crimes and kick them out if they mess up. The Electoral College is a safety net against dictators in the U.S. History shows why this matters: Hitler was made leader of Germany without being elected, and Mussolini got power in Italy because people liked him. The Electoral College, made up of normal folks, stops this from happening here. It's not a perfect democracy, but it represents democracy in our kind of government. Richard A. Posner said the Electoral College isn't democratic in the modern sense; electors choose the president, not directly the people. Governments can be awful. While the U.S. government usually tries to do what's best for people, some want to get rid of the Electoral College. Even Nixon, who had to quit because of scandals, once wanted to. People like Bradford Plumer say it's unfair and old. But the Electoral College is like a watchdog, making sure elections are fair and giving the final vote through people who represent both states and their people. It's representation in how we vote.

The Electoral College represents the people and was created by the Founders for a reason. Despite being controversial, many respected presidents, like Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson, were elected through it. To keep freedom safe from dictators and protect the people's power, the U.S. must keep the Electoral College.

Senator, I think the electoral college, even though it might have been okay a long time ago, is just too old-fashioned now. People in the U.S. should get to pick their president themselves. The way it is now isn't fair, it's kind of dishonest and just plain wrong. Some people might say life's not fair anyway, but we should still try to make our country as fair as we can, especially with how much debt we have. And, in my opinion, that debt got even worse when Obama was president. It's not right that some people's votes count more just because of where they live; it makes the whole system unequal.

It makes Americans feel small when electors in the electoral college have so much power. We get to vote for these electors, but they often don't even care about what the majority wants. They don't just go against what the people want, they also make us lose faith in our government. If we get to vote, we should also get to vote on whether the electoral college should even exist. I bet a lot of people feel the same way. I know I'm young, and you might not think I know much, but please listen to what the whole country is saying.

Like Bradford Plumer said, we don't really get to vote for our president directly. Remember the 2000 election, when Al Gore got more votes but still lost? If we're all supposed to be equal, why do electors get special treatment? It's like we're being tricked into thinking our votes matter, when they really don't. If electors are the ones who are supposed to choose the president, then why even have a public vote?

Some people say the electoral college stops one group from having too much power, but that's not really how it works. The way the electoral college is set up, where whoever wins the most votes in a state gets all of that state's electoral votes, is what really decides the election. Why are these electors better than regular people? I might be young, but I'm just as smart as my parents. Why should their votes be ignored? I think the U.S. is built on lies and isn't fair. We're told we're in charge, but the people we elect and the electoral college are really the ones in power.

America is going downhill, and it's going to keep going that way unless we change things. Getting rid of the electoral college would help our country do better. Why should you listen to a teenager? Because I know what it's like to not be valued, even if you're good at things. America knows what's best for its people. A few people in each state shouldn't get to decide for everyone else. Let us vote for the president directly, just once. If it doesn't work out, you can always go back to the old way. But remember, if we don't try to do better, we'll never get anywhere.

Lots of folks just think having a car is normal, but it's still a big deal. All over the place, people are driving every day, but tons don't have a car or haven't even driven one. In Vauban, a fancy spot in Germany, folks decided to ditch cars. Besides the train to Freiburg and a few roads around the edge, there aren't cars in Vauban. You can have a car, but parking is super limited. You gotta buy a parking spot in a big garage way outside town for a crazy 40,000, plus the cost of your place! So, 70% of Vauban folks don't have cars, and 57% sold theirs when they moved. Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mom, said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Vauban, done in 2006, shows a growing thing in Europe, the US, and other places to split up living in the suburbs from needing a car. That's a big part of "smart planning."

Elisabeth Rosenthal said in her article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," that cars can be good and bad. Cars make air dirty and hurt the planet. In Paris, the air was so bad they stopped people from driving for a bit. One Monday, cars with even numbers on their plates couldn't drive, or they'd get a 31 fine. The next day, it would've been odd numbers. The bad air was worse because of the temperature; the dirty air from cars got stuck under warmer air. After just five days, there was way less traffic, like 60% less! The dirty air was as bad as in Beijing, China, which is known for being polluted. The air got better on the day they first stopped cars, so they let odd numbers drive the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, also tried going car-free with a "Day Without Cars." People were told to walk, bike, skate, or use buses. The streets had no traffic, and there were no accidents! This wasn't just one time; it was the third year they did it. About 7 million people were supposed to find other ways to get around. Even though it rains a lot in Bogota, lots of people still joined in. The mayor, Antanas Mockus, said, "The rain hasn't stopped people from participating." Many said that the car-free day made them less stressed and made the air better. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman riding a bike with his wife, said, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution."

In the US, everyone has a car and drives all the time. Researchers are asking if we're done driving so much. The US is big and has lots of suburbs, so it's always been about cars. It's where the Model T came from, where Detroit is, and what inspired "Mustang Sally." But maybe America doesn't love cars as much anymore. By April 2013, people drove almost 8% less than before, like it was January 1995 again. The bad economy made it worse because people couldn't buy cars and didn't need to drive to work. Michael Sivak, a professor at the University of Michigan, said that "rates of car ownership per household and per person started to come down two to three years before the downturn," which means "something more fundamental is going on." We need other options to stop people from going back to driving all the time. New York's bike programs and higher tolls, plus car-sharing everywhere, show that things are changing. A study from the year before said that young people drove 23% less from 2001 to 2009.

Lots of folks are trying to drive less for different reasons. They're worried about safety, how much it costs, and the environment.

Most people get in a car and drive a lot each week. I drive to school, sports, and friends' houses. Some say driving is good because it helps us travel and do things, but too much driving is bad. It might seem hard to stop driving so much, and some might not think it's helpful, but there are many good things about driving less. Less driving means cleaner air, better health, and more happiness. That's why leaders are trying to get us to drive less.

Beijing has the worst air pollution because of too many cars, said Robert Duffer in 2014. (source 2 paragraph 14) Paris had bad air too, so they made a rule: cars could only drive on certain days based on their license plates. If you broke the rule, you had to pay a fine. (source 2 paragraphs 10-19) This rule helped Paris's air get better quickly, so they stopped the rule after one day. This shows that driving less is good. Elizabeth Rosenthal said in 2009 that cars cause a lot of pollution in Europe and the US. (source 1 paragraph 5) We need to drive less to have cleaner air and use less gas.

Driving to work might seem easy, but driving less can make us happier and healthier. Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, where most families don't own a car. (source 1 paragraph 3) Heidrum Walter, who lives in Vauban without a car, said she feels less stressed now. (source 1 paragraph 3) In Bogota, Columbia, they banned cars for a day, said Andrew Selsky in 2002. (source 3 paragraph 21) Carlos Arturo Plaza in Bogota said it was nice to relax and have less pollution. These people show that driving less makes us feel better, so we should drive less.

Driving less is so good that many people want us to drive less. The EPA supports communities where people drive less, and leaders are doing things to help. (source 1 paragraph 9) David Goldberg said that we've built everything around cars since World War II, but that needs to change. Mimi Sheller said that things are changing, and we're starting to see a big shift. (source 4 paragraph 35) This support shows that driving less has many benefits, so we should drive less.

Our world is changing. Young people are driving less now than before. (source 4 paragraph 41) Now is a good time to stop driving and take buses, bikes, or trains. If we drive less, we help keep the air clean and become happier and healthier. That's why the EPA wants us to drive less. We need to act now to protect the future. Because there are so many good things about driving less, we should drive less.

To my Florida senator, listen up! I'm just a student, not some political whiz, but I gotta tell ya, I'm all in for keeping the Electoral College. Seriously, this old-school method just makes picking a president way smoother and faster, you know?

Okay, so there are 538 electors in the Electoral College, and a candidate needs like, 270 votes to win the whole shebang. Sure, some folks whine that we're not really voting for the president directly, but for electors who are into them. Well yeah, kinda, but voters are also backing the whole shebang, like the candidate's master plan. We vote for the ideas a candidate promises. The system shines the spotlight on winning those swing states, which I think is important. As mentioned in the article "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," those swing states tend to be way more involved in the election. This could prevent repeated elections since smaller states will not matter.

Most states go with the "winner-take-all" deal, handing over all their electoral votes to the candidate who wins the popular vote there. But some states use a "proportional representation." I believe that the "winner-take-all" system is the best. While other stuff about the Electoral College might be whack, this part just makes it easier to give all of a state's votes to one person, so things stay calm within the state. Unlike proportional representation, this speeds up the presidential election.

Last thing, the Electoral College has been around forever. According to "What Is the Electoral College?", "The founding fathers established it in the Constitution as a compromise between election of the President by a vote in Congress and election of the President by a popular vote of qualified citizens." This tradition is very important to our country's roots. I appreciate you reading my concerns.

Dear esteemed state senator,

I believe we must change the Electoral College and switch to choosing presidents by popular vote. We should all have the right to vote directly for a president who can make things better for us. Right now, we vote for electors who then pick the president, which doesn't seem fair. A presidential election should let everyone participate, not just some people. So, we need to fix how we vote.

Think about it: if people are voting in a presidential election, why should they have to vote for electors who then vote for the president? "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2) says the Electoral College is unfair because candidates only focus on "swing" states they might win. Also, "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) says no region has enough electoral votes to elect a president on its own. This shows how the Electoral College hurts some regions because their votes might not matter, and the president might ignore them.

Also, everyone, not just citizens, should have the right to vote. When many people can't vote because they're not citizens, it doesn't truly show what the people want. This is like how some regions don't have enough electoral votes. It creates an uneven playing field where some people's voices are louder than others. Source 3 says that voters in "tossup" states pay more attention because they know their votes matter. But it's not fair to limit voting to only those "thoughtful voters" when others want to vote too.

On the other hand, the Electoral College does have some good points. It makes voting easier for electors. If there's a tie, the House of Representatives decides, with each state getting one vote. This system is already in place and has solutions for problems. But even though popular vote might seem to have issues, we can fix them. Also, the Electoral College can lead to some regions not having enough electors.

In short, the United States should start electing its president by popular vote. This could change our society for the better and give everyone a chance to participate and feel like they matter.

Across the globe, it's clear that lots of people get around using their own cars. Still, this reliance on cars is a big problem because it hurts the environment and makes people super stressed out. So, if we could find ways to use cars less, it could really make a difference for the better.

If we didn't depend so much on cars, the air in busy cities would be much cleaner. The stuff that comes out of car exhaust pipes is full of nasty chemicals, mostly from the gasoline, and that makes the air really polluted. Experts point out that "passenger cars are responsible for 12% of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe and up to 50%...in the United States"Source 1. This really shows just how much cars mess with the environment. Take Paris, for example. They use a ton of cars, and they ended up with crazy-high levels of smog because of all the car fumes. Reuters reported, "Paris had 147 micrograms of particulate matter per cubic matter compared with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London"Source 2. But after they banned cars for a while, the smog finally started to clear up. So, it's pretty obvious that car fumes are seriously bad news for the environment.

Plus, if we didn't rely on cars so much, it could make our communities way less stressful. In Vauban, Germany, they've mostly banned cars, which means the streets are nice and quiet. One of the people who lives there, Heidrun Walter, said, "When I had a car I was actually tense. I'm much happier this way,"Source 1. That really shows how peaceful things can be when you get rid of cars. And in Bogota, Columbia, they have a "Day Without Cars" where nobody's allowed to drive. According to Andrew Selsky, this has led to improvements like, "pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks" and "rushhour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic"Source 3. So, cutting back on car use can really take the pressure off people who are always dealing with driving and traffic jams.

Even though cars are a handy way to get around, they're not all sunshine and roses. The fumes they make contribute to global warming, and being stuck in traffic can drive anyone crazy. If we could just use cars less or even ditch them altogether, it would definitely help make the planet a healthier place.

Senator, I'm writing to share worries about the Electoral College. I think the way we pick presidents is unfair. Sure, it has some good points, but I believe the bad outweighs the good. In my view, the Electoral College is just plain wrong and needs to go.

The Electoral College has 538 electors. This could lead to a tie, even though it hasn't happened yet. History shows us that a few votes could have changed things. Richard A. Posner said that a tie is possible because 538 is an even number, but it's very unlikely. Surprisingly, only a few votes are needed to cause a tie. Back in 1976, just a few voters in Ohio and Hawaii voting differently would have tied the election. These numbers might seem big, but they're small compared to the total number of voters. Even though the number of electors per state is based on its size, a tie is still a worry that could be avoided if we just counted the popular vote.

Plus, electors are citizens with the right to vote. But this can cause problems. Politics can be tricky. In 1960, Louisiana almost used the system to pick electors against John F. Kennedy because they liked the other guy. Posner said that parties pick electors they trust to vote for their candidate, and they usually do. Still, the electoral vote winner can lose the popular vote. Electors can technically vote for whoever they want, which is unfair. Voters might feel like their vote doesn't matter. It's true that one vote might not change everything, but candidates need to appeal to everyone. They often don't bother campaigning much in states they know they'll win because no one region can elect a president. This can make voters feel unimportant and not want to vote. If the winner was picked by the most votes, people would feel like their vote matters and would be more likely to vote.

Remember the 2000 election? There was a fight over the popular vote. Confusion about votes in a state that decides the electors can cause trouble. When a candidate gets more votes, it helps them and hurts those who voted for the other person. Posner also talks about the problems with runoff elections. Some candidates might not get a majority of votes, which makes things complicated and leaves the decision to the Electoral College.

Basically, the Electoral College seems wrong because of all the potential problems. The US should use a better, fairer way to elect our president, where every vote counts the same. The current system can lead to ties, broken promises, and fights over close votes. We should switch to a system where the popular vote decides the president, so everyone's voice is heard. Thanks for thinking about this important issue.

Esteemed Senator, I pen this to share my conviction, echoed by many, that the Electoral College is a crucial institution deserving preservation. Sure, it's not perfect, but its advantages for both state and national elections are clear. It's a representative voting style, rooted in common impact, leading to a more solid and trustworthy election.

First off, regular folks choose the Electoral College electors. This makes sure folks with similar views and values get picked to speak for the public, upping the chances their votes mirror what people want. Plus, each state gets a rep count based on its population. These electors, not holding office, are often just average Joes who get the public's needs. If picked well, they'll fight for their state's best stuff. So, the Electoral College gives a more targeted way to vote.

Now, some say the Electoral College has risks, like electors ditching their promises. But that's not just an Electoral College thing. Presidential candidates make promises all the time, and no one can say they'll keep them all. The Electoral College needs trust, too. Like trusting a candidate to put the nation first, you gotta trust electors to do their job right. Trust is key in any election.

Lastly, the Electoral College makes the election outcome more certain than just a popular vote could. A tied election? Chaos! But the Electoral College, with its rep system, can break that tie, helping a state or party out. It has some luck involved, but it's better than flipping a coin. Bottom line: The Electoral College makes voting more reliable.

In short, we gotta keep the Electoral College. It's a more focused voting method, needs no more trust than other elections, and boosts the whole system's reliability.

Cars have turned into the go-to way to travel far. More and more, people need cars for everyday trips. A lot of folks are getting lazy, skipping bikes or even skates for short hops. Instead of walking to the corner store, more people are driving. All this car use really messes up the air, causing problems like bad health and hotter temperatures worldwide. The point is, cutting back on cars is a great idea.

If we cut down on cars, maybe by saying how often people can drive, we could clean up the air a lot and get people moving more. Vauban, a place near France and Switzerland, tried going car-free early on, banning street parking, driveways, and home garages. Because of this, most families there don't have cars, and a lot sold their cars to move there. One resident, Heindrun Walter, said she was happier without a car, enjoying the quiet streets with bikes and kids playing, instead of car noise.

Paris, famous for its smog, has also worked to reduce car use. "After days of really bad pollution, Paris made some driving illegal to clean up the air." Because of these rules, "Traffic jams were way down in Paris, after five days of thick smog..." By making cars less common, people have to find other ways to get around, like biking or walking. This gets people moving and lowers the risk of serious sicknesses, like cancer, diabetes, and breathing or heart issues. Using cars less can help fix or stop global warming, keeping the sea from rising and protecting the ozone layer.

"BOGOTA, ColombiaIn a plan that's spreading, millions of Colombians walked, biked, skated, or took the bus to work on a car-free day, leaving the city streets empty of traffic." These fun activities are a way to get where you're going while being active. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza, riding a bike with his wife, said it was a good way to relax and clean up the air. According to Mimi Sheller, a professor, things are changing, showing we're seeing a long-term shift. The world is changing, affecting our health and the environment.

Driving less can mean cleaner air and healthier people. If we make fewer cars and use them less, people will probably pick other ways to travel, like bikes, skates, or running. Now is the time to make this change. Many cities have started this, and it's time for everyone to join in, one step at a time.

The Electoral College is still argued about a lot in American politics. This system decides who becomes president. However, most people – about 60%, some say – want to get rid of it and use a simple popular vote instead. But others really want to keep the Electoral College. The way it works means someone can win the presidency even if they didn't get the most individual votes. So it should be swapped for popular vote instead.

Each state gets a certain number of electoral votes. They all start with two, and then get more based on how many people live there – from three to fifty-five, according to other sources. A candidate needs 270 out of the 538 total electoral votes to win. Usually, whoever gets the most votes in a state gets all of that state's electoral votes. Maine and Nebraska are the exceptions. Because of how it’s set up, some people feel their vote doesn't matter and don't even bother to vote, especially if they belong to a political party that doesn't win in their state. Those who like the Electoral College say it makes sure there's always a clear winner. It also stops someone from winning just by appealing to one area of the country. Also, it can guarantee a result even if the popular vote is super close.

At first, the Electoral College was there to protect the smaller states. Now, it doesn't really do that anymore. Presidential campaigns mostly concentrate on swing states to try to get their electoral votes. Candidates don't really bother with states that always vote for one party because they already know who's going to win there. If the system isn't working as planned, then maybe we shouldn't keep using it.

Basically, the Electoral College is old and doesn't work well for picking the president. Even though it usually picks a winner, there could still be a tie. Some say that focusing on swing states makes voters more informed and helps them make better choices. They think people in swing states know more because their votes matter more. However, candidates would just focus somewhere else if we got rid of the Electoral College, and that would end the swing states' special influence. The fact that some votes are worth more than others is a bad thing. So, getting rid of the Electoral College is the smartest thing to do.

Dear Senator, the Electoral College's worth has made people argue a lot. It's caused stress for both everyday Americans and those in power. This system, which picks our President, also makes sure everyone's vote counts the same. When we became a democracy, we promised to keep the government and the people in check. Without the Electoral College, this balance would be at risk. It's super important for keeping the government strong but also making sure regular folks have a say.

The Electoral College is good for the country in a bunch of ways that people don't always see. For example, if you want to win, you have to get to know people all over the country. Source 3, line 19 says, "You need to appeal to different regions." You can't just win by being popular in one area. This keeps things fair nationwide, so one part of the country doesn't get too powerful. Plus, it makes sure people know who they're voting for and what it all means.

Being equal is really important. It's what makes our country strong. If we stick with the Electoral College, we're saying we want the best system possible. Source 1, line 1 says, "It's not a place, it's a process. The founders made it up to find a middle ground between Congress picking the President and the people picking the President." This shows why it's so important to keep things balanced.

Also, the Electoral College usually means the person who wins is actually qualified, based on the votes. Source 3, line 22 says, "It stops elections where no one gets most of the votes." This helps us pick a President in a way that's easy and makes sense. Some people might complain that it doesn't really represent the people because of how the "electors" vote. But that's not really proven, and we can argue against it. How can people vote right if they don't have support?

So, the Electoral College is super important for keeping things balanced in our country. People count on this balance a lot. Getting rid of it would hurt our country, the future, and everyone who lives here.

I say we should get rid of the Electoral College, because it messes things up more than just counting all the votes would. So, I think we should switch to a system where whoever gets the most votes wins, instead of sticking with this old Electoral College thing. It's really worrying when state governments start picking electors themselves, because those electors might not even do what the people want. Sure, the Electoral College has some good points, but the bad stuff is way worse.

The big problem with the Electoral College is that it's not straightforward. We don't actually vote for the president directly; we vote for these electors who then pick the president. If a certain group of electors wins in a state, all that state's electoral votes go to their candidate. Like, if a winning group in Florida supports a particular candidate, that person gets all 29 of Florida's electoral votes. But, things can go wrong, especially when elections are super close. If there's a tie, the House of Representatives has to decide, and each state only gets one vote. This long, drawn-out process is why I don't like the Electoral College. If we just counted all the votes, we could be done in days, not weeks or months.

Basically, the Electoral College screws over voters. Because most states give all their votes to whoever wins there, candidates only focus on the states where the race is tight, ignoring everyone else. It's not right to make people go through all this when we could just count the votes and be done with it. Getting rid of the Electoral College would make elections faster and calm things down in the country, so we wouldn't have so many political fights.

Honestly, I think the Electoral College is unfair, old-fashioned, and doesn't make sense. The reasons people give for keeping it aren't very good, and the reasons against just counting the votes are even worse. Maybe there are some arguments for keeping the Electoral College, but the risks of doing that are bigger. Switching to a popular vote would make picking the president easier and make the country happier by causing less drama.

To the State Senate, I'm writing to push for a big change: switching how we pick our president from the Electoral College to just counting up everyone's votes. I've really dug into the Electoral College, learning all about how it works and what people think of it. This letter is about why we shouldn't keep the system we have now and why a popular vote is the way to go.

The Electoral College is tricky. Posner points out it's not a direct vote where everyone gets a say. Instead, "it is the electors who elect the president, not the people... you're actually voting for a slate of electors." You're not voting directly for a person. You're voting for a group who has promised to vote for that person. The Office of the Federal Register backs this up, saying, "The Electoral College is a process, not a place," like some building or office. It's how the process works.

Plumer brings up a scary thought: "Perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." If that happens, the House of Representatives decides who wins. But here's the rub, "Because each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." So, a small state has the same power as a huge state. It's super unfair. The article also mentions the 2000 election, where Gore got more votes overall, but Bush won because of the Electoral College. Is that really fair? Plumer also says "...the electoral college is unfair to voters...swing states..." Candidates just focus on a few states. Plumer concludes, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational," because of ties, unfair representation, and just being messed up.

On the flip side, some folks like the Electoral College because of "...Certainty of Outcome...Everyone's President...Swing States...Big States... Avoid RunOff Elections..." (Posner). But those reasons don't hold up. The 2000 election was anything but certain. The "Everyone's President" idea isn't true because electors vote, not us. Swing states get all the attention, leaving others out. Posner says it helps big states, stating that "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution..." But that's still unfair. Finally, even though Posner thinks the Electoral College "avoids that problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the votes cast," it's not fair when some votes count more than others, all because of the swing state thing.

Basically, ditch the Electoral College for a popular vote. The current system is a mess. The 2000 election shows how bad it can be. Swing states get all the love, and ties are a nightmare. It's just biased, doesn't make sense, and is totally unfair. Some people disagree, but the reasons to switch to a fair, democratic vote are way stronger.

It's practically expected in America: adults own cars. A lot of folks think they're a must-have – how else ya gonna get around? But hold on, there are plenty of ways besides driving, and chances to just use our cars less. Cutting back on car use is getting super popular lately. Yeah, there are some downsides, but the good stuff way outweighs them!

Pollution's a big problem all over the world, and cars are a huge part of it. It messes with our health and the planet. Paris tried banning some cars to fight nasty smog. Turns out, after just five days of smog, traffic jams went down by 60 percent in Paris, says Source 2! Just one day of fewer cars made a big difference. Imagine how much better things would be if we all drove less for longer – weeks, even months! We don't have to ditch cars forever, just be smart about when we use them.

Using cars less can also chill us out and make us feel good. Sounds weird, right? Wouldn't other ways of getting around make us MORE stressed? Take Vauban, a town in Germany. They kinda "banned" parking, driveways, and houses with garages. They put shops and stuff close enough to walk to easily. Heidrun Walter, a media coach and mom, said in Source 1, "I was always stressed with a car. I'm way happier now." If a busy mom can do it, lots of us can. Being stuck inside all the time, dealing with kids and everything, can make us tense and feel crazy busy. When walking is easier than paying a ton for parking, we're way more likely to go outside, hang out with people, and get some fresh air! That sounds way better than being stuck in a car all the time.

Less driving is a great idea, no doubt! I'm all for using my car less. But until it's easier to get to places we usually NEED a car for, it'll be tough for everyone to jump on board.

For many years, cars have been how folks mainly traveled. But now, cars aren't so great because they cause issues. Cutting back on cars could be a big help. People would move around more, there would be less pollution, and people and cities could save money.

One good thing about using cars less is it gets people moving. When we always drive, we don't get much exercise. Cars replaced walking, biking, and skating, which are now just for kids. In places like Vauban, Germany, where there aren't many cars, you hear kids playing and bikes riding. This shows that less cars mean more activity. Also, Bogota, Columbia's "Day Without Cars" worked really well. After three years, they made over 118 miles of bike paths. So, less car use definitely helps people be more active.

Also, less cars would mean way less pollution. Billions of cars make a ton of pollution. In Europe, cars make up about 12 percent of the greenhouse gases. In the U.S., it's closer to 50 percent! This pollution, mostly in big cities, is making the air bad. Not much pollutes as much as cars. Power plants are one of the only things that do. Less cars would help the environment by lowering pollution. Basically, cars cause a lot of pollution, and cutting back would be good for the Earth.

Lastly, using cars less would save money for people and cities. Keeping up with cars and buying gas costs a lot for car owners. In April 2013, people drove 9 percent less than before, back to 1995 levels. This happened because many Americans didn't have enough money to buy new cars. Owning a car is expensive for regular people. Cities also spend a lot. In the big six-year transportation bill, most money went to highways (80 percent), and only a little (20 percent) went to other transportation. This is making lawmakers worried. So, driving less is helpful because cars cost a lot for everyone.

Envision a world devoid of cars. Think of streets completely empty, absent of traffic, no loud horns, just peace and space. A lot of folks would see this as a more chill community. And honestly, using cars less could really help the world. Fewer of those big, fast cars on the roads could be good for the planet, save a bunch of cash, and make things safer.

It's cool that car companies are pushing hybrid cars and trying to make cars that don't hurt the planet as much. But, the biggest thing we can do for the Earth is just have fewer cars in general. Studies show that greenhouse gases can be "... up to 50 percent in some car-intensive areas in the United States." Plus, there's more pollution, carbon, and smog. Paris even had to stop people from driving for a bit to deal with smog. "...Last week Paris had 147 micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London...". Those numbers are going up in Europe and the US. Think about how bad things could get in twenty years if this keeps up. Getting rid of a lot of cars could drop those levels a ton, stop bad gases from messing up the environment, and clean up the air.

Everybody loves saving money, like with coupons or deals. Imagine saving thousands every year. If you drive less, you could save money by taking the bus, not paying for car insurance, and not having to own a car. Some people might think not having a car is hard, since they need to get around. But in Vauban, Germany, cars are pretty limited, and people are doing fine. Reports say that "Vauban, home to 5,500 residents within a rectangular square mile may be the most advanced experiment in lowcar suburban life". Even though cars are restricted, people can walk, bike, or take the tram. And now, lots of places in America have carpool apps, buses, and van services. Instead of spending so much on gas, car payments, and insurance, try sharing a ride or getting some exercise!

When you see someone drive off, do you ever worry if they'll be okay? Stats show that car deaths and injuries happen way more than accidents with other ways to get around. If there are fewer cars, there will be less drunk driving, fewer crashes, and fewer people getting hurt or killed. It's way too common to hear about teens driving drunk and causing terrible accidents. If we focus on buses and trains, we can stop these crashes and keep young people safer. Also, traffic can make people stressed and angry, which makes accidents more likely because people drive crazy. Walking instead of driving can help stop these things, plus you get fresh air and exercise.

Basically, cars have been good and bad. They help us get around, but they also hurt the planet, cost us money, and aren't always safe. If we use cars less, we can help the environment by lowering greenhouse gases, pollution, and carbon. We can also save a lot of money by not having to pay for car stuff. And, fewer cars can save lives by stopping drunk driving and crashes, which means fewer deaths and injuries. Next time you're walking around, think about what it would be like if there were no cars.

Many places are trying to cut back on how much people drive all over the world. Some have made "no-car" zones or only let cars drive sometimes. They even have "Car-Free Days" now and then! A big reason for this is to fight pollution, especially from those stinky diesel trucks. This movement is getting popular in places like Vauban, Germany; Paris, France; and Bogotá, Colombia. They are starting to see good results and hoping others will join in too!

In Vauban, Germany, the fancy people who live there mostly don't own cars. You can't park on the street or have other car stuff in this "new test area." You can own a car if you live on the edge of town, but parking is super limited. If you want to park, you have to buy a spot in a big garage way out there. It can cost up to $40,000!

It looks like people are happier without cars in this part of town. Instead of loud cars, they like hearing "bikes and kids playing." Vauban is known as a great example of "smart planning." When it was done in 2006, most of the people who moved there sold their cars!

Vauban shows how Germany has been trying to make suburbs that are easier to walk, bike, or take the bus for the past 20 years. About 5,500 people live there in just one square mile. It's seen as one of the best examples of a suburb where people don't drive much. Germany wants to make it easier to get around without a car. That's why stores are on the main streets near houses instead of far-away malls you can only get to by highway.

Even though Germany is trying to make towns without cars, some people still want those big suburbs with garages like in New York. While some folks still like the ideas from the 1950s, many places are starting to do things more like Vauban.

Paris, France, stopped some people from driving to fight smog that was as bad as in Beijing, China! The city told drivers with even-numbered license plates to not use their cars. If they did, they had to pay 22 euros (about $31 USD). The next day, drivers with odd-numbered plates couldn't drive either.

Because of this rule, almost 4,000 cars got towed because people broke the rules! But traffic in the city went down by almost 60%! French officials said diesel cars were the problem, even though they have a tax that makes diesel cheaper than gas. That's why 67% of cars in France are diesel.

Because there are so many diesel cars, Paris has some of the worst air pollution in Europe. The driving ban had some problems, like delivery companies losing money. But there were good things too! Electric cars, hybrids, and cars with three or more people could still drive. Public transportation was also free during the ban. In the end, the air got clean enough that officials let odd-numbered cars drive again after a few days.

In Bogotá, Colombia, they started a "Car-Free Day" and wanted other countries to do it too! Millions of people walked, skated, biked, or took the bus to work that day. Colombia has had Car-Free Day for one day each year for three years now. If you drive that day, you have to pay a $25 fine. In a city with 7 million people, the goal is to cut down on smog and get people to use other ways to get to work, school, and home.

Even though it was rainy, more people participated than expected. Two other cities in Colombia joined in for the first time too! Officials from other countries came to see what was happening in Bogotá. They were excited and said that the people of Colombia were making a big change that would spread everywhere!

This idea has been growing since the mid-1990s. Bogotá has built 118 miles of bike paths, more than any other city in Latin America. They have also made lots of parks and sports areas. They have replaced small sidewalks with wide, smooth paths for people to walk on. New fancy restaurants and shops have popped up because of Car-Free Day.

Studies show that Americans are buying fewer cars and not driving as much. This matches goals that President Obama had set before. Since the United States buys more cars than almost any other country, it looks like people are starting to not love cars as much because they are worried about pollution.

One reason for this is probably the recent recession. Lots of Americans couldn't afford new cars, and it looks like that's still happening. Because of this, big car companies are having money problems.

Companies like Ford and Mercedes are changing what they do and calling themselves "mobility companies" now. They are making different kinds of products. Not as many young people (ages 16 to 39) are getting driver's licenses either. Many are sharing rides, walking, or taking the bus. They don't think they need a license as much anymore.

Many countries are trying to fight pollution by making laws and stopping people from driving sometimes. They are seeing cleaner air because of it. These countries are doing something to fix big problems that cause even bigger problems like global warming. Things are changing, and it's helping everyone!

Senator, I believe the Electoral College is still the best way to elect leaders, given its historical track record. It has advantages such as the focus it puts on swing states, its system of giving electoral votes based on state size, and how it stops a president from winning with just support from one area.

Swing states, because of the Electoral College, help voters think more carefully. People in these states know their vote matters, so they're more likely to really think about who to vote for. This makes it more likely that the best candidate wins. Voters in swing states tend to study the candidates more. A popular vote system, without the winner-takes-all method, would make swing states matter less.

Also, the Electoral College assigns votes based on state population. Presidential candidates want to win big states because they have more votes. A popular vote could lead candidates to spend time in smaller states, ignoring the big ones. The current system makes sure elections are fairer, stopping less populated states from having too much power.

Plus, the Electoral College prevents a candidate from winning only because they're popular in one area. Candidates have to campaign everywhere, not just where they're already liked. With a popular vote, candidates wouldn't know where to focus their campaigns, because the winner-takes-all is gone. The Electoral College makes it harder for a candidate with mainly regional support to win, as other areas would not feel as important.

Because of all this, I think the Electoral College is better than a popular vote for presidential elections.

The Electoral College? It's old news for picking presidents. Seriously, why are we still using it? America needs to switch to a system where the person with the most votes wins, plain and simple.

Okay, so the winner-take-all thing seems easy, but is it really fair? The Office of the Federal Register says that most states do this: the candidate who gets the most votes in the state gets all of its electoral votes. Some say that if a candidate works hard in a state, they deserve all the votes if they win, even by a little bit. High risk, high reward, right? That's the argument for keeping it. Plus, that first article? It mentions each candidate has electors, chosen by their party, who actually vote. So, we're voting for *them* to vote for the candidate? Nope. People should directly choose who they want. End the Electoral College.

Bradford Plumer's article, "The Indefensible Electoral College," nails it. We're not *really* voting for president, but for electors who then pick the president. Every vote should count the same! It's not right to give some votes more power just because of who someone is. Plumer says, "If you lived in Texas... you'd vote for a slate of 34 Democratic electors pledged to Kerry." Why should Kerry get 34 votes just because his party picked those people? He should only get votes from people who *chose* him! If they don't like him, he shouldn't get their vote. All votes equal: ditch the Electoral College.

Plumer also brings up the scary thought of a tie. Then what? The House of Representatives decides, with each *state* getting one vote. Seriously? Wyoming, with hardly anyone, gets the same say as California with millions? That's not equal! A popular vote makes ties almost impossible. Also, Plumer points out, "Al Gore... won the popular vote but lost the presidency." The guy most people wanted didn't win because of this Electoral College thing! Bush became president. The Electoral College is just messed up and causes way more problems than it solves.

We need to go popular vote, ASAP. It's better for everyone, and way more fair to the candidates. The Electoral College has got to go.

It's undeniable: Cars are essential to modern living, almost like food or shelter. But while cars make life easy, people often forget the bad effects of so many cars. This writing will look at those effects, pointing out the problems and ways to fix them.

A big worry is how much cars add to greenhouse gasses around the world. In Europe, cars make up about 12 percent of these gasses. Vauban, Germany, is trying to fix this. This special town is trying to be "car-free," which means changing the town a lot. This idea is catching on, with plans to make closer, smaller suburbs that are easy to get around by bus or train. "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way," said Heidrun Walter, a resident. This shows that some people find it hard to change, but others find life gets better.

Smog is another problem caused by cars in big cities. Paris had so much pollution it had to stop people from driving to clean the air. Diesel fuel, which was cheaper because of a French tax law, makes the smog worse, meaning the French government needs to change its rules. However, new data shows that Americans are buying fewer cars, getting fewer licenses, and driving less. If this continues, it could help the environment because America makes a lot of pollution. The internet also helps because young people can talk to each other without driving. "Pedestrian, bicycle, private cars, commercial and public transportation traffic are woven into a connected network to save time, conserve resources, lower emissions and improve safety," said Bill Ford, Executive Chairman of the Ford Motor Company.

Bogota, Colombia, has a program to get people to use other ways to get around and cut down on smog. According to businessman Carlos Arturo, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." The city has more sports centers and parks, fixed old sidewalks, made rush-hour less strict, and added nice shopping areas. Other countries are watching this experiment closely.

These are just some of the good things that happen when people use cars less. These early results show that things could get even better if more people and towns tried to rely less on cars.

To pick the U.S. president, folks vote for who they like best. But, the person with the most votes nationwide doesn't always win. We use something called the Electoral College instead. Basically, whoever gets the most votes in a state wins all of that state's "electoral votes," which are then cast by a group of people. This system? I think it stinks, and lots of others do too. It kinda messes up who gets heard, makes people not want to vote, and makes candidates ignore some states.

One big problem with the Electoral College is that it doesn't give everyone a fair say. States with more people get more electoral votes. Like, Wyoming has only about 500,000 voters, so they barely get any electoral votes. Because of that, each voter's opinion is less important when you're trying to get the 270 electoral votes you need to win. That's just not good enough. Plus, sometimes the person who wins the most votes overall still loses the election, like Bush vs. Gore back in 2000.

Some say it's fair to base electoral votes on how many people live in each state. But I think a national popular vote – where whoever gets the most votes nationwide wins – would be way fairer. In states where one party is super popular, like Texas with the Republicans, that party usually wins every time. So, if you're a Democrat in Texas, you might feel like your vote doesn't matter, and you just won't bother voting. That's not fair to those voters, and it's not really what America's about.

When a state gets more people, it gets more Electoral College votes. So, candidates really focus on a few "big states" to try and win them over. The other states, the ones with fewer votes, often get ignored. When candidates travel around giving speeches, some states get totally skipped. In the 2000 election, for example, candidates didn't even bother showing up in seventeen states. That makes people in those states even less likely to vote.

So, yeah, the Electoral College is messed up. It doesn't represent people well, it makes them not want to vote, and it encourages candidates to only care about the big states. Switching to a national popular vote would fix all that and make presidential elections way better.

Dear Senator,

I'm writing to you today to talk about the Electoral College (EC) and its good and bad sides. This system, the way we pick our president and vice president, worries many Americans. Some say it's old and unfair, saying the person with the most votes from everyone should just win. While not having direct control over who becomes president can be upsetting, the Electoral College does have some advantages. For example, it can help sort out problems and make things easier during voting, and it can lower the number of votes needed for a clear result. Also, it tries to give a voice to people in different parts of the country. But, we have to wonder if these good things are worth the bad. Is making things easier a good enough reason to have a gap between citizens and their president? Most say the Electoral College is like a safety net, meant to make voting less complicated. Yet, many American voters don't like this use of the EC, seeing it as a threat to what the United States stands for. So, the Electoral College, as it works now, shouldn't be allowed to continue.

The idea of the EC being a backup to fix voting issues is really a problem. In theory, the EC makes sense, but how it's used often leads to mistakes that hurt how voters are represented. When people vote, they're actually voting for a group of electors, chosen by each party and expected to back their candidate. Richard Posner, in his writing, "In Defense of the Electoral College," admits that the Electoral College winner might not always get the most votes from everyone. But, he quickly says that this doesn't happen often. Posner is right that it's rare. This is because the EC mostly works on a "winner-take-all" basis, where the top candidate gets all of a state's electoral votes. To see the problems with this, we need to think about the ethics. The president should represent most of the people, and of course, some won't be happy. To represent the largest group of Americans, the candidate needs to get the most votes from everyone. A smart citizen learns about the candidates and decides who best fits their beliefs. By voting, they're saying that a certain candidate, if elected, would represent them best. If over half the people pick a candidate, that candidate should logically represent the majority of Americans. But, the EC gives all of a state's electoral votes to the candidate who wins the most electors in that state. If one candidate wins 55% of the electors' votes, while another gets 45%, the candidate with 55% wins all the electoral votes for that state. So, people who would be properly represented by a popular vote might not get true representation from the EC. This gap between American citizens and their president is bad for the whole country, including the president.

A common complaint about the Electoral College is that it keeps United States citizens away from the presidential candidates they choose. This has been defended by those who say that "the EC avoids the problems of polling every American." Polling everyone supposedly has risks, like the huge number of votes making it hard to count accurately. But, accurately counting votes is the job of the president, who, even near the end of their time, must still represent those who voted for them by allowing them to pick their president of choice. Here, the excuse of "practicality" just means they don't want United States citizens to vote. There's a good chance of problems in popular voting, but less chance of problems in the Electoral College, because fewer electors are polled. The EC tries to give an accurate picture of the population, making sure the chosen candidate is best for most of each area, not just one area. Sadly, even with these efforts, the EC system still isn't accurate. In his writing, Posner says, "the most thoughtful voters should decide the election," and his thinking makes sense. An uneducated voter is a risk to any society, no matter the nation. But, the United States promises every citizen the right to vote. A powerful person can't just say that "only educated voters should decide the election," because an educated voter isn't easy to define, and can't be reasonably defined.

The EC's mistakes are also made worse by big states. While it can be said that bigger states have more people, so a candidate chosen by popular vote represents those people no matter where they live, smaller states argue that states, as separate entities, should have equal say in the Senate and in the EC. Each state was given two senators, no matter its size, because of the Senate's plan. However, the Electoral College doesn't give equal representation to smaller states. A candidate's win through popular vote depends on the population of a state. The EC also depends on a state's population. Since smaller states don't have many electoral votes, it could be said they should have an equal vote in the Senate of the United States Congress. But, the EC doesn't offer this balance.

Understanding the source of this problem is key to making real change. Accuracy should be achieved when possible, and it should be aimed for when it seems impossible. By any measure, letting the EC continue as is lacks accuracy, and gives up both accuracy and representation for the sake of electoral ease. A well-set-up Electoral College should see itself as helping, not fighting, the popular vote and the people's representation. Senator, I've saved my personal feelings for the end, but I have to say I'm not happy with the EC as it works now. I know some practical things can't be changed, and I get that the EC might be easier for hiring people to track votes and run the process. But, finding the most accurate method is vital and must be worked toward. Senator, you were elected by popular vote because people chose you as the candidate who best matched our needs and would likely defend those needs against others. Just as we've supported you with our votes, we now ask you to represent us in picking our presidential candidate, one who will best serve our country's needs. Because of the reasons above, we strongly support getting rid of the Electoral College for a more exact voting system.

Sure, while taking the car on those long road trips is an obvious need, there are other ways to get around when traveling locally, like taking the bus or bike. There are plenty of gains to not taking the car so often. Yes, cars are handy, but there are other less expensive ways to travel.

For one, there are transit systems that combine public and private ways to get around. These save time and stuff, limit pollution, and just make getting around safer. Cars lead to accidents from people getting tired, not paying attention, or when there is no place to park. And, it's a big deal to own a car with bills like gas and repairs piling up. When money is tight, the bus or subway is a way more affordable plan. And if the car gets towed, that really makes things worse. When the weather traps smog, the car exhaust sticks around making pollution worse. Some folks act like you need a car to get by, but with public transit, they are wrong.

Plus, cutting down on car usage helps the earth. At first, the roads might seem packed, but it really does cut down on smog when people aren't driving all the time. Car-free days really show how things get better when people drive less, cutting stress and cleaning the air. All this leads to big upgrades. We get parks and places to play, better sidewalks, less traffic when people are rushing, and new places to shop and hang out. We're used to driving in the US, but the hard times we've seen lately might keep folks from rushing back to driving to work with all these awesome changes.

To wrap it up, having a car is a big load, and while it's needed sometimes, not all the time. Yes, cars are important but we also can't kill the earth. Yeah, cars are a breeze, but you can totally go places without them and it's cheaper too.

The never-ending din of car alarms, blaring horns, and roaring engines? It's the new normal, the soundtrack to our lives. And all those cars? Seriously, it's getting to be too much.

Let's be real, we should all try to drive less.

Think about it, doing that could actually be pretty great.

Sure, cars feel important, but a lot of the time they are extra. For a big trip? Okay, maybe. But walking ten minutes to the store? Come on!

Cutting back on car use matters, seriously. A family spends a ton on gas each week – like $20 to $50! One good thing about driving less? Major savings on gas, money that could feed your family.

"When I had a car, I was always stressed. Life is better now," one person said. People ditching their cars? They often seem way happier.

Cleaner air is another win! And that means we all get healthier.

"It's great for stress and helps the air," said Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, riding a bike with his wife. People who drive less? They're usually pretty happy about it.

If more people saw how great it is to drive less and really tried to, the air would be cleaner, and we'd all be happier.

One person who used to drive all the time said her kids "... plan their summer jobs and fun stuff around walking, the bus, or rides with friends." Plus, her kids aren't even rushing to get their licenses, even though a car would be handy where they live. People are even having fun walking instead of driving!

Driving isn't evil, but driving less? It's actually good for you. You save a lot of money by walking, the air gets better for everyone, and people can focus on what's important: work, friends, and family.

Try it! Park the car and leave the keys. Walking might be surprisingly fun, a nice change from driving.

Dear Senator, as a student, I'm urging a big change to how we elect presidents: ditching the Electoral College for a national popular vote. Switching to a popular vote would let each voter directly pick the president, instead of risking a weird, indirect system deciding things. I truly think every citizen should vote and directly affect the election, without complex electors messing things up. This change is key to avoiding past mistakes and fixing the unfairness of the "winner-take-all" deal.

See, the current system messes up because it makes voters vote for electors, not the president directly. How electors get picked – by state parties or the candidates themselves – weakens voters' control. We deserve a louder voice in choosing our leader and shouldn't be scratching our heads about what our votes even mean.

Plus, we could have a "disaster," like when Louisiana almost messed up its electors in the '60s. That close call shows how the Electoral College can be manipulated and cause chaos. Keeping this system means we risk repeating similar problems with how electors are chosen.

Lastly, the "winner-take-all" part of the Electoral College twists presidential campaigns. Candidates only focus on a few swing states, ignoring voters in places they think are already decided.

Basically, the Electoral College is old news and should go. It ruins direct representation, risks old crises, and makes campaigns unfair with the "winner-take-all" thing. We must be fair to voters who don't get the system, dodge old mistakes, and fix the illogical "winner-take-all" setup.

Because it wedges electors between the people and those running for president, the electoral college is not good. People don't get to pick the president directly. Instead, they pick electors who then do the voting. This might make people unsure about what they're actually voting for. It would be better if we just counted everyone's votes because the electoral college isn't really democratic.

The idea was to give smaller states a fair say, but big states still have too much power. Some states have lots of electoral votes, but there are way more states with only a few votes. All those little states together can have more influence than the big ones.
