If someone couldn't afford a car, a bike might have been easier to buy for getting to work or looking for a job. But some people might be too lazy to choose biking or walking. Also, owning a car costs a lot for gas and insurance.

Luckily, it seems like Americans don't love cars as much anymore. As families had more kids, people drove less and less in 2005. Also, roads are more dangerous with cars. There are fewer young drivers (16-39 years old), and older people are keeping their licenses for longer. A study said that young people were driving 23% less between 2001 and 2009. Bill Ford wants to work with phone companies to make cities where walking, biking, cars, trucks, and buses all work together in a smart way to save time, use less stuff, pollute less, and be safer. In Germany, they banned cars in some places, and now 70% of families don't own cars, with 57% selling their cars before moving there. If we make these changes, we can help fix the air and protect our planet.

Cars are everywhere, used by tons of people all over the world to get around. They make life easier and more useful, helping with daily errands and travel. We often hear about how great it is to have a car, but we don't always think about the bad stuff. Even though cars can be handy, depending on them too much can cause more problems than it solves. Using cars a lot can really hurt the environment and make people feel super stressed, even if they don't know it. So, it's important to really think about how much we rely on cars.

The environment gives us everything we need to live, like air and food. But cars can be a big problem for it. They release carbon dioxide, which pollutes the air. In Robert Duff's article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," he talks about how Paris had such bad air pollution from CO2 that they had to stop people from driving for a while. This was because lots of people used diesel cars, which was encouraged by tax breaks. Duff says that "diesels makes up 67 percent of vehicles in France, compared to.3 percent average of diesle engins in the rest of Western Europe, according to Reuters." Duff also points out that cities with lots of people often have the worst air pollution, like Beijing. Cars definitely have a bad effect on the environment, which is why people are trying to find ways to use them less. Elisabeth Rosenthal's "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars" tells the story of Vauban, Germany, where many people live without cars. In Vauban, 70 percent of families don't own cars, and 57 percent sold their cars when they moved there. You can still own a car, but you have to pay a lot for a parking spot – around 40,000. People prefer to walk or bike instead. Vauban shows that it's possible to reduce the amount of pollution cars create.

Besides hurting the environment, cars can also make people stressed without them even knowing it. Heidrun Walter, in "In German Suburb, Life Goes on Without Cars," says she's much happier without a car: "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Also, businessman Carlos Arturo said that a car-free day in Bogota was "a good opportunity to take away stress an lower air pollution." Cars can take over people's lives, and they might not realize how much of an impact cars have until they stop using them so much.

Cars have changed in importance for people. Before, cars were just for getting around. But, because tech keeps getting better, fewer people are driving in some places. You might think less driving is bad, but it's actually good in many ways.

The USA has always loved cars. They made the Model T and inspired the song "Mustang Sally." President Obama hoped that Americans would change their habits to help lower pollution. He said in "The End of Car Culture" that things were changing. Studies show people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and getting licenses less often. By 2013, people were driving almost 9% less than before. Also, fewer young people were getting licenses. This is because of the internet, which lets people work from home, cities getting better, and phones and carpool apps. It's not that cars are old news, but life is just easier now than in the 60s because of tech. Unlike the U.S., where driving is a choice, places like Paris don't let you drive as much.

Paris, France is a beautiful place, but it has a lot of smog. The article "Paris bans driving due to smog" says they stopped some people from driving to make the air better. If people drive when they're not supposed to, they have to pay 22 euros. They only let cars with certain license plates drive on certain days. The article says that traffic went down by 60% after they did this. But some cars could still drive, like hybrids and cars with three or more people. Diesel cars, which are most of the cars in France, were making the most pollution. Cars that used gas weren't as bad. Like Paris, Bogota, Colombia is also trying to fix its air.

Colombia has a day where nobody can drive. It's a 24-hour ban, which is stricter than in Paris. If you drive, you have to pay 25. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" says that "millions of Colombians hike, bike, skate, or take buses to work during the carfree day." Like Paris, Bogota wants people to use other ways to get around and stop smog. A businessman named Carlos Arturo Plaza said it's "a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." Driving can be stressful because of road rage, which can ruin your day. Driving is good in some ways, but there are other options. Colombia wants to "promote alternative transportation and reduce smog." Cars keep getting better, but even though they used to be super important, now people see them as less important. They're mostly just for getting around when you don't have other choices.

If fewer people used cars, the air would get cleaner, that's one big plus. Cities are super polluted because of cars, especially in busy areas. Paris even had to stop people from driving sometimes because the pollution was so bad, like one source said. This pollution heats up the earth and makes it hard for people to breathe. After a bad smog week, Paris stopped some cars, and traffic went down a lot—60 percent! The pollution gets worse because it's cold at night but hot during the day.

Another good thing about driving less is you get more exercise. Walking or biking becomes necessary, and it's a great workout. Plus, you meet people while you're out and about. When people walked more, gyms and parks got busier, and new stores and restaurants popped up and made more money.

More and more people are skipping cars and using buses, bikes, or their own two feet. Studies show that young people used cars way less—23 percent less between 2001 and 2009. If more people shared rides or used other ways to get around, there would definitely be less pollution everywhere. If we cut down on car pollution, the air would be much cleaner, and it would be easier to breathe.

All the money spent on roads and traffic lights could go to better things if people didn't drive so much. Even though lots of people rely on cars, there are always other options. Using cars less also saves cash, since gas prices are always changing.

Basically, there are tons of reasons to drive less or not at all. It helps the planet's air and gives you exercise. You can walk, bike, or take the bus for cheap. You also save money on gas, car repairs, and maybe even buying a car in the first place.

Lots of folks in the U.S. drive cars all the time, but there are other ways to get around that people forget about. So, what's so good about finding new ways of traveling? Well, ditching the car habit has some big upsides.

If we used cars less, stores could pop up closer to home, you know? Instead of sprawling malls by the highway, shops could be right down the street, easy to walk to. This would mean new buildings, stores, and houses all close enough to walk to.

Walking to stores? That's cool! No need for a car as much. And if we don't drive as much, we'd probably walk and bike more, which is way healthier. Studies show that walking every day is good for your brain.

Also, less driving means cleaner air. Cars cause air pollution, which makes smog, and that's bad for everyone's health. So, driving less means less stress and cleaner air.

Cars pump out a lot of greenhouse gases. In Europe, cars make up 12% of those gases. But in some U.S. spots with tons of cars, it can be as high as 50%! Cutting back on cars helps us and the planet too.

We don't have to drive everywhere! We can get around in other ways that are good for us and the Earth. With so many good things coming from less driving, why not give it a shot and make our lives and the world better?

Envisioning a world sans automobiles might seem unrealistic. Yet, these visions are becoming reality, demonstrated by new towns, city driving limits, and national car-free days. Cars cause a lot of the world's pollution, a fact too often ignored. We must face our planet's destruction and act.

Vauban, Germany, is an interesting example. It has a strange rule: residents must give up their car or pay a huge $40,000 parking fee. Thus, a report states that "70% of Vauban families don't have cars, and 57% sold their cars to move here." Heidrun Walter, a mom and media trainer, said, "I was always stressed with a car. I'm much happier now." This is "Smart Planning," separating suburbs from car dependence. Cars make up 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, and 50% in the U.S. We can lessen these emissions and breathe better air with Smart Planning.

Paris, France, shows car pollution issues too. A source noted, "After days of crazy pollution, Paris banned some cars to clean the air." This cut traffic by 60% over five days. Diesel fuel, used by 67% of France, caused the smog. Climate played a role; "cold nights and warm days trapped car emissions," the report mentioned.

Europe is not alone. Bogota, Colombia, had its third "Day Without Cars," allowing only buses and taxis. Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "It's a great chance to reduce stress and pollution." Two more Colombian cities, Cali and Valledupar, joined the Day Without Cars for the first time this year.

Car use is changing in the U.S. too. Miles driven peaked in 2005, but have been falling since. "In April 2013, miles driven per person were almost 9% below the peak, like January 1995," a source says. Less car use helps the environment but challenges car makers. Ford and Mercedes are adapting as "mobility" firms. New York City has a bike-sharing program, and bike paths are being built, with tunnel tolls showing the shift.

Cars have hurt the earth a lot, and we must fix it. With these new, eco-friendly ideas, we're moving towards a future without cars running our lives.

It's tough to say for sure what's right about the Electoral College. It's been around since the start, but its original reasons don't make as much sense now. Back then, most people didn't have much schooling to make smart choices when voting. But now, things are different! People are way more educated, and we've got tons of news and ways to learn about who's running for president. Shouldn't the people get to pick the president, not just politicians? The president should work for us, not just the political insiders.

Even folks who like the Electoral College have to admit it's complicated. When we vote for a president, we're actually voting for a group of people called electors. Each state picks them differently. These electors promise to vote for a certain candidate and then vote for president. But, as Bradford Plumer says, we can't control how these electors actually vote! Plumer also points out that some voters "get confused" about electors and vote wrong. And sometimes, these "faithless electors" don't even vote for the person they promised to!

Also, candidates focus on "swing states." These are states where neither party has a lock on the vote, so they can change the whole election. Since the Electoral College gives all of a state's votes to the winner, if you're a Democrat in a super Republican state, you might not even bother trying. You'll probably lose anyway. So, campaigns spend almost all their time in swing states. But, if we just counted all the votes across the country, campaigns would have to pay attention to everyone, everywhere!

If we picked the president by popular vote, it would be more fair. Even smaller groups in states that usually vote one way would have a say. Plus, a poll once showed that most voters would rather just pick the president directly. Even someone who defends the Electoral College admits that it could end in a tie. He even says that one vote doesn't matter, which is silly! If everyone thought that way, nobody would vote, and elections wouldn't work at all! Every vote matters, and every group deserves to be heard. A popular vote is the best way to pick a president who will help all Americans. It should be for all people. So, let everyone vote!

Post-World War II, cars became super important in everyday life. Sources 1 and 4 show how some people are driving less or not at all by choice. On the other hand, sources 2 and 3 talk about cities that have banned cars on certain days. When fewer people drive, there's less pollution and it's easier to get around town.

Air pollution is a big problem everywhere. In source 1, Rosenthal says that "cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases... and even 50% in some car-heavy U.S. areas P5." Because so many people use cars, cities often have smog. When Beijing hosted the 2009 Olympics, they cut down on car use and the air got much better. They had to do this to protect the athletes and visitors from lung damage. People in Beijing already wore masks to avoid breathing bad air. While it was a drastic step, limiting cars prevents terrible outcomes. Duffer says in source 2 that Paris, a big city, has much less pollution now P19. The city even started banning cars based on license plate numbers. Less car pollution would mean cleaner air and better views.

Even though the current generation grew up with cars, many are waiting longer to get their license or not getting one at all. Selsky mentions that when there are fewer cars, more people use parks and sports areas. Shopping centers and pedestrian zones are also more popular P28. Because fewer people are driving, new businesses and cool restaurants are popping up. Instead of big malls by highways, shopping areas are closer to public transportation or within walking distance. Rosenthal mentions that he and his kids use public transport, carpools, and walking instead of driving P40. Public transport is now viewed as a smart way to travel in the city. Carpooling is now considered a valuable resource for everyone involved. Thanks to carpools, walking, and public transport, it's now easier to get around and explore the city.

Cities are changing their design to focus on people who don't have cars. There are many benefits to restricting car use. Cleaner air and easier transportation options mean cars are becoming less necessary. Cutting down on car usage will probably lead to even more advantages later on.

The electoral college: what *is* it? It's like, electors get picked, then they meet and vote for the president, and those votes get counted. The real question is: good for us, or leading to a total mess? It's gotta go, 'cause it's just not fair for a direct election and could end badly.

For one, some think the electoral college stops those annoying runoff elections. They say it might make things easier for the government. Richard A. Posner said when no one wins most votes, people want runoffs. Keeping the electoral college, they claim, means fewer runoffs. But, is it really a good fix long-term, and what's the risk? Since electors vote for president, should we trust them to stop runoffs? Posner notes it does reduce the pressure to further complicate the presidential election process. Still, this one pro doesn't make up for all the cons of the electoral college.

Plus, it could be a total disaster, which is a big problem. As the writer states, Americans should be glad the 2000 election was the worst in a century because Plumer says it could be much worse. It's happened before. Plumer thinks the electoral college has hurt the U.S. a lot, and keeping it could mean more problems. The writer also notes that electors have sometimes voted against their party's choice for someone else, says Plumer. That shows it's not just useless, but maybe even able to be messed with. It must be axed because it could really screw up our government.

On top of the whole disaster thing, it's not even a direct election. The Office of the Federal Register says when you vote for a candidate, you're really voting for their electors. Meaning you don't really get to pick who runs the country. It feels wrong that people's lives are changed by leaders they didn't vote for, and those leaders make big decisions. Plumer calls it unfair, outdated, and dumb. These issues prove the electoral college lowers faith in government. Plumer says candidates skip states they won't win, focusing on "swing" states. This shows winning is more important than the country's good. Will we just let it abuse its power and maybe destroy us?

So, yeah, in short, the electoral college? Get rid of it.

Dear Senator from Florida, the founding fathers designed the Constitution with electors. They thought these folks would mirror what the people wanted when picking the President. But, this system can lead to a candidate winning the popular vote but still losing the whole shebang, it is old, and can lead to a tie. A lot of people in the U.S. think we should follow democratic rules, and this system may work against that.

The popular vote shows what Americans really want. A big chunk of people, "over 60 percent," would rather just vote directly for the President, showing that they want their vote to have a bigger impact. Take for instance, when the President sends troops to places like Afghanistan, Iraq, and Iran. Voters want their choice of President and their say in this matter, to actually matter instead of being overshadowed by the electoral college. The 538 electors, even though they're relatively small in number, have more clout than the votes of millions of citizens. Remember, the Declaration of Independence mentions that we have "unalienable rights, that among these are: life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness" and liberty is tied to the right to vote.

The electoral college has been here for a long time, but that’s the problem. To make sure the U.S. does not fall behind, we need to adjust the rules of the Constitution. Society changes, so our systems should change for the sake of doing better. As proof, "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century," highlighting a past vulnerability that could come back to haunt us.

To make matters worse, the chance of a tie makes the problems even bigger. It is said that, "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." A tie like that does not align with our democratic ideals. A democracy should be all about the popular vote, not a standoff in the legislature. Per Source 2, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe.” The popular vote could be ignored in a tie, making things dicey.

In the end, we should scrap the electoral college because it is hurting U.S. citizens. It makes the popular vote less important, it is out of touch, and there is a risk of a tie. I am hoping that the president will be elected by the will of the people, not the folks in the capital.

Okay, so cars? They're for errands, right? Picking up folks, hitting the shops. But imagine, no cars at all. Weird, huh? Actually, some good stuff might come from that.

First off, think about the planet. Cars stink it up! Like, "Passenger cars? They cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases!" And get this, "Up to 50% in some crazy car-loving spots in the US!" (That's from "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 5). No cars means clean air, less smog. Our air? Way better!

And get this: we'd be healthier, too. Cleaner air, duh. But also, no cars kinda forces you to walk or bike. "Vauban," says "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," paragraph 6, "with its 5,500 peeps in a small area, is trying out no-car life." Stores are close, like "walkable close", not "miles away in some mall." So, people walk, bike, take the bus. More moving, less sitting. Good stuff!

Plus, cars might be losing their cool factor. "Obama's goals to cut US emissions," states "The End of Car Culture," paragraph 29, "are helped by people buying fewer cars, driving less, and getting fewer licenses." Gas prices suck, texting and driving is scary, new drivers are clueless. People are just not into cars as much. That's great for the earth! Maybe driving just won't be that important soon.

Basically, fewer cars? Win-win! The environment gets a break, and it looks like folks are already ditching their rides. Why drive a gas-guzzler when you can walk or bike and get fit? Less driving means good times!

For quite some time, the Electoral College has sparked debate, drawing both praise and disapproval. The question is whether to get rid of it or keep it as is. Some say it's unfair and undemocratic, treating voters and candidates poorly. But others think it's the best way to pick a president. Both sides have valid reasons, so should we change it, and if so, how?

The Electoral College is a middle ground for picking the President, not just by popular vote. In each state, a party picks a group of electors. So, when you vote for a candidate, you're really voting for the electors who support them.

Why do some want to ditch the Electoral College? Well, it's got some serious problems. The biggest one might be a tie in the electoral vote. How could that happen? Electors could go rogue and vote for someone else. When you vote, you're indirectly picking these electors, but they might not always do what you expect. This could really shake things up if electors don't vote for their party's candidate. Back in 2000, after Al Gore won the popular vote but lost the presidency, many voters wanted direct elections. It seems a lot of people aren't fans of the Electoral College.

Still, some folks want to keep the Electoral College. Each party picks electors they trust to vote for their nominee, and usually, they do. When a party picks its electors, it's likely to choose people who will back their candidate. Also, it's about regional appeal. If a president only wins big in one area, they probably won't be a great president. If they only care about one region and ignore the rest, they won't address everyone's needs. In the end, they probably won't get elected. Lastly, when no candidate wins a clear majority, the Electoral College can pick a definite winner.

So, both sides have strong points about the Electoral College's future, whether to keep it or scrap it. But, in the end, it's up to you, the people, to decide.

Cars are a big deal for many people every day, like taking kids to school or sports. But more and more people think using cars less can fix a lot of problems.

Everyone knows cars are bad, especially for the planet. Dirty air and greenhouse gasses are big worries. In Europe, cars make about 12% of those gasses. In places in the US where everyone drives, it can be as high as 50%. Also, traffic makes things worse. For example, in France, diesel cars made smog really bad in cities. When it was cold at night but warm during the day, the smog got stuck. Using cars less means less bad air, which is better for everyone.

Cutting back on cars also helps people, not just the environment. People all over the world have tried going without cars and using other ways to get around. Usually, they like it. In Vauban, Germany, a rich mom named Heidrun Walkter got rid of her car. Now, she likes walking and hearing bikes and kids. She said, "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I'm much happier." If we use cars less, we also move around more. In Bogota, Colombia, millions of people walked, biked, or took the bus on a day when cars were banned to help with smog. It worked, and people felt less stressed. There was no traffic, and people felt closer. One guy biking with his wife said it was good for the planet and for them.

So, using cars less definitely makes the air cleaner and cuts down on smog around the world. You hear more kids and bikes. From Germany to Paris, Colombia, and the US, countries are trying to fix the problems caused by driving too much. They're always working to make things better.

Dear state senator, I'm writing to really push you to back a popular vote system for picking the U.S. President. I think this switch is super important for three big reasons. One, electors might just ignore who the people actually voted for. Two, we could end up with a tie that sends the whole election to the House. Three, the winner-take-all thing is just plain unfair.

First off, electors have gone rogue before, and they could totally do it again. Like Bradford Plumer said in "The Indefensible Electoral College," some folks in Louisiana almost messed up the 1960 election by swapping out electors. And sometimes, individual electors just vote however they want, not how their party told them to.

Also, the idea of an Electoral College tie is scary. If that happens, the House of Representatives decides, with each state getting one vote. So, a rep from Wyoming, with like 500,000 people, would have the same power as a rep from California, with almost 35 million! Back in 1976, a tiny shift in votes in Ohio and Hawaii could've caused a tie. We've been way too close to that.

Lastly, the Electoral College's winner-take-all setup is just not fair to voters. Candidates only care about swing states and ignore the rest. In 2000, seventeen states got zero love from the candidates. And tons of voters in big media markets didn't even see any campaign ads. It's hard to justify letting a small bunch of voters in places like Ohio decide who's president.

So, state senator, I'm saying again, please vote for a popular vote system for presidential elections. Remember that electors can go against the voters, a tie sends things to the House, and the winner-take-all thing is just messed up.
